import Game.Metadata

World "FinTuples"
Level 21

Title "Boss: Two Strategies, Two Tuples"

Introduction "
# Boss: Proving Tuples Are Equal

This boss has two parts, testing both reconstruction strategies.

**Part 1 â€” Front reconstruction**: You're given tuples `f` and `g`
described in different ways:
- `f` by its head and tail: `hf0 : f 0 = 1`, `hft : Fin.tail f = ![2, 3]`
- `g` pointwise: `hg : forall i, g i = i.val + 1`

Use the **cons reconstruction** from Level 20 to determine `f`,
then `ext` + case split to verify `f = g`.

**Part 2 â€” Back reconstruction**: You're given a tuple `p`
described by its init and last element:
- `hpi : Fin.init p = ![10, 20]`
- `hpl : p (Fin.last 2) = 30`

Use the **snoc reconstruction** (`vecSnoc_self_init` + `.symm`)
to determine `p = vecSnoc ![10, 20] 30`.

This boss combines:
- `Fin.cons_self_tail` + `.symm` + `rw ... at` (front reconstruction)
- `vecSnoc_self_init` + `.symm` + `rw ... at` (back reconstruction)
- `ext` + nested `cases` (pointwise verification)
- `rw` with a universally quantified hypothesis
"

/-- Two tuples reconstructed from different ends. -/
Statement (f g : Fin 3 â†’ â„•)
    (hf0 : f 0 = 1) (hft : Fin.tail f = ![2, 3])
    (hg : âˆ€ i : Fin 3, g i = i.val + 1)
    (p : Fin 3 â†’ â„•)
    (hpi : Fin.init p = ![10, 20])
    (hpl : p (Fin.last 2) = 30) :
    f = g âˆ§ p = vecSnoc ![10, 20] 30 := by
  Hint "The goal is a conjunction. Use `constructor` to split
  into Part 1 and Part 2."
  constructor
  -- Part 1: front reconstruction + pointwise verification
  آ· Hint "Part 1: determine `f` using cons reconstruction.
    `have hf : f = ![1, 2, 3] := by`
    then inside: `have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm`
    followed by `rw [hf0, hft] at key` and `exact key`."
    have hf : f = ![1, 2, 3] := by
      have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm
      rw [hf0, hft] at key
      exact key
    Hint "Good! Now use `ext âں¨v, hvâں©` to reduce `f = g` to
    checking each index."
    ext âں¨v, hvâں©
    Hint "Rewrite `f` using `hf` and `g` using `hg`:
    `rw [hf, hg]`."
    rw [hf, hg]
    Hint "Now case-split on `v` to verify each index."
    Hint (hidden := true) "`cases v with | zero => rfl | succ n => ...`"
    cases v with
    | zero =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "Both sides evaluate to 1. Use `rfl`."
      rfl
    | succ n =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "Continue: `cases n with | zero => rfl | succ m => ...`"
      cases n with
      | zero => rfl
      | succ m =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "`cases m with | zero => rfl | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)`"
        cases m with
        | zero => rfl
        | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)
  -- Part 2: back reconstruction
  آ· Hint "Part 2: determine `p` using snoc reconstruction.
    Use `have key := (vecSnoc_self_init p).symm`."
    have key := (vecSnoc_self_init p).symm
    Hint "Now substitute the known last element and init:
    `rw [hpl, hpi] at key`."
    rw [hpl, hpi] at key
    Hint "Now `key` matches the goal. Use `exact key`."
    exact key

Conclusion "
Congratulations â€” you've completed **Fin Tuples**!

Here's your tuple toolkit:

| Tool | What it does | Learned in |
|---|---|---|
| `![a, b, c]` | Build tuple with vec notation | Levels 1-2 |
| `use` + `constructor` | Construct and verify a tuple | Level 3 |
| `Fin.cons a f` | Prepend element to tuple | Level 4 |
| `Fin.cons_val_zero` | `Fin.cons a f 0 = a` | Level 5 |
| `Fin.cons_val_succ` | `Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i` | Level 5 |
| `Fin.tail f` | Drop the first element | Level 6 |
| `Fin.cons_self_tail f` | `cons (f 0) (tail f) = f` | Level 7 |
| Round-trip | `tail (cons a g) = g` and `(cons a g) 0 = a` | Level 8 |
| `vecSnoc f x` | Append element to tuple | Level 9 |
| `vecSnoc_last` / `vecSnoc_castSucc` | Access snoc'd elements | Level 9 |
| `Fin.init f` | Drop the last element | Level 10 |
| `vecSnoc_self_init f` | `vecSnoc (init f) (f (last n)) = f` | Level 11 |
| `ext i` | Reduce function equality to pointwise | Level 12 |
| Tuple composition | `g circ f` transforms tuples | Level 13 |
| Tuples as data | Verify concrete computations on tuples | Level 14 |
| Cons injectivity | Equal cons'd tuples have equal parts | Level 15 |
| Snoc injectivity | Equal snoc'd tuples have equal parts | Level 16 |
| `Fin.elim0 i` | Eliminate impossible `Fin 0` | Level 17 |
| `ext` + case split | Prove `f = ![...]` by index | Level 18 |
| `.symm` | Flip an equation | Level 19 |
| Reconstruction | `.symm` + `rw at` from head/tail or init/last | Level 20 |

**Proof strategies**:
- **Index-by-index** (`ext` + `cases`): when you know each value
- **Front reconstruction** (`cons_self_tail.symm` + `rw`): when you know head + tail
- **Back reconstruction** (`vecSnoc_self_init.symm` + `rw`): when you know init + last

**The big picture**: `Fin n -> alpha` models ordered tuples. `Fin.cons` builds
from the front, `vecSnoc` from the back. Both have decomposition (tail/init)
and reconstruction (cons_self_tail/vecSnoc_self_init) identities. Function
extensionality (`ext`) lets you prove two tuples are equal index by index.
The round-trip identities (cons then tail gives back the same tuple, and
vice versa) mean that `Fin.cons` and `(head, tail)` form a **bijection**
between `alpha x (Fin n -> alpha)` and `Fin (n+1) -> alpha`. In later
worlds, you'll formalize this idea as an `Equiv` (equivalence).

**Where this is going**: Tuples are the building blocks for **big operators**
like $\\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} f(i)$, written `\\sum i, f i` in Lean. Summing the
entries of a tuple `f : Fin n \\to \\mathbb{N}` is exactly what big operators
do â€” and that's where this course is headed after Finset.

Next up: the Pset (problem set) world, which will test these skills
on fresh problems without scaffolding.
"

     2â†’
     3â†’World "PsetFin"
     4â†’Level 18
     5â†’
     6â†’Title "Boss: Phase 1 Complete"
     7â†’
     8â†’Introduction "
     9â†’# Boss: Integrating All Fin Skills
    10â†’
    11â†’This boss has two parts that draw on all three lecture worlds.
    12â†’
    13â†’**Part 1 â€” Comparing two tuples**: `f` is described by its head
    14â†’and tail, `g` by a pointwise formula. Prove they're equal.
    15â†’
    16â†’Strategy: first **reconstruct** `f` from head + tail (FinTuples
    17â†’Level 19 recipe), then use **ext + case split** to compare
    18â†’with `g` index by index.
    19â†’
    20â†’**Part 2 â€” Non-constant tuple**: Prove that `f` has at least two
    21â†’different values.
    22â†’
    23â†’Strategy: provide a witness index where `f` differs from `f 0`.
    24â†’
    25â†’Moves required:
    26â†’- Front reconstruction (`.symm` + `rw ... at`)
    27â†’- Function extensionality (`ext`)
    28â†’- Case analysis with impossible-branch elimination (`absurd`)
    29â†’- Universally quantified rewrite (`rw [hg]`)
    30â†’- Existential witness (`use`)
    31â†’- Inequality pattern
    32â†’"
    33â†’
    34â†’/-- Comparing reconstructed tuples and finding non-constant entries. -/
    35â†’Statement (f g : Fin 3 â†’ â„•)
    36â†’    (hf0 : f 0 = 2) (hft : Fin.tail f = ![4, 6])
    37â†’    (hg : âˆ€ i : Fin 3, g i = 2 * i.val + 2) :
    38â†’    f = g âˆ§ âˆƒ i : Fin 3, f i â‰  f 0 := by
    39â†’  -- Step 1: Reconstruct f
    40â†’  Hint "First, determine what `f` actually is.
    41â†’  Use the front reconstruction pattern:
    42â†’  `have hf : f = ![2, 4, 6] := by`
    43â†’  then inside, start with `have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm`."
    44â†’  have hf : f = ![2, 4, 6] := by
    45â†’    Hint (hidden := true) "`have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm`
    46â†’    gives `key : f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f)`.
    47â†’    Then `rw [hf0, hft] at key` substitutes the known parts.
    48â†’    Finally `exact key`."
    49â†’    have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm
    50â†’    rw [hf0, hft] at key
    51â†’    exact key
    52â†’  Hint "Good! Now `hf : f = ![2, 4, 6]`.
    53â†’  Split the conjunction with `constructor`."
    54â†’  constructor
    55â†’  -- Part 1: f = g
    56â†’  آ· Hint "Show the functions agree at every index.
    57â†’    Use `ext âں¨v, hvâں©`, then `rw [hf, hg]` to substitute both."
    58â†’    ext âں¨v, hvâں©
    59â†’    Hint (hidden := true) "After `rw [hf, hg]`, the goal at each
    60â†’    index reduces to a numerical equality. Case-split on `v`
    61â†’    and use `rfl` for each valid case."
    62â†’    rw [hf, hg]
    63â†’    cases v with
    64â†’    | zero => rfl
    65â†’    | succ n =>
    66â†’      cases n with
    67â†’      | zero => rfl
    68â†’      | succ m =>
    69â†’        cases m with
    70â†’        | zero => rfl
    71â†’        | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)
    72â†’  -- Part 2: f has non-constant values
    73â†’  آ· Hint "Find an index where `f` differs from `f 0`.
    74â†’    Since `f = ![2, 4, 6]`, any index besides 0 works."

import Game.Metadata

World "PsetFin"
Level 18

Title "Boss: Phase 1 Complete"

Introduction "
# Boss: Integrating All Fin Skills

This boss has two parts that draw on all three lecture worlds.

**Part 1 â€” Comparing two tuples**: `f` is described by its head
and tail, `g` by a pointwise formula. Prove they're equal.

Strategy: first **reconstruct** `f` from head + tail (FinTuples
Level 19 recipe), then use **ext + case split** to compare
with `g` index by index.

**Part 2 â€” Non-constant tuple**: Prove that `f` has at least two
different values.

Strategy: provide a witness index where `f` differs from `f 0`.

Moves required:
- Front reconstruction (`.symm` + `rw ... at`)
- Function extensionality (`ext`)
- Case analysis with impossible-branch elimination (`absurd`)
- Universally quantified rewrite (`rw [hg]`)
- Existential witness (`use`)
- Inequality pattern
"

/-- Comparing reconstructed tuples and finding non-constant entries. -/
Statement (f g : Fin 3 â†’ â„•)
    (hf0 : f 0 = 2) (hft : Fin.tail f = ![4, 6])
    (hg : âˆ€ i : Fin 3, g i = 2 * i.val + 2) :
    f = g âˆ§ âˆƒ i : Fin 3, f i â‰  f 0 := by
  -- Step 1: Reconstruct f
  Hint "First, determine what `f` actually is.
  Use the front reconstruction pattern:
  `have hf : f = ![2, 4, 6] := by`
  then inside, start with `have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm`."
  have hf : f = ![2, 4, 6] := by
    Hint (hidden := true) "`have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm`
    gives `key : f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f)`.
    Then `rw [hf0, hft] at key` substitutes the known parts.
    Finally `exact key`."
    have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm
    rw [hf0, hft] at key
    exact key
  Hint "Good! Now `hf : f = ![2, 4, 6]`.
  Split the conjunction with `constructor`."
  constructor
  -- Part 1: f = g
  آ· Hint "Show the functions agree at every index.
    Use `ext âں¨v, hvâں©`, then `rw [hf, hg]` to substitute both."
    ext âں¨v, hvâں©
    Hint (hidden := true) "After `rw [hf, hg]`, the goal at each
    index reduces to a numerical equality. Case-split on `v`
    and use `rfl` for each valid case."
    rw [hf, hg]
    cases v with
    | zero => rfl
    | succ n =>
      cases n with
      | zero => rfl
      | succ m =>
        cases m with
        | zero => rfl
        | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)
  -- Part 2: f has non-constant values
  آ· Hint "Find an index where `f` differs from `f 0`.
    Since `f = ![2, 4, 6]`, any index besides 0 works."
    Hint (hidden := true) "`use âں¨1, by omegaâں©` provides the witness.
    Then `rw [hf]` substitutes the known tuple, and the
    inequality between `4` and `2` closes automatically."
    use âں¨1, by omegaâں©
    rw [hf]
    intro h; cases h

Conclusion "
Congratulations â€” you've completed **Phase 1: Finite Types**!

Here's everything in your toolkit:

**Construction**: `âں¨k, by omegaâں©`, `use`, `![a, b, c]`, `Fin.cons`, `vecSnoc`

**Extraction**: `.val`, `.isLt`, `Fin.val_last`, `Fin.val_succ`, `Fin.val_castSucc`

**Equality**: `ext` (for Fin or functions), `Fin.ext_iff`, `rfl`

**Inequality**: `intro h; cases h` (literals), `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h; omega` (general)

**Case analysis**: `cases x with | mk v hv =>` + nested `cases v`

**Reconstruction**: `Fin.cons_self_tail.symm` (front), `vecSnoc_self_init.symm` (back)

**Impossible cases**: `exact absurd hv (by omega)`

**Why do we need more than `Fin n -> alpha`?** Tuples are powerful
â€” but they're *ordered* and *indexed*. Not everything in mathematics
has a natural ordering or an index. The set of prime divisors of 30
is `(2, 3, 5)` â€” but why should 2 come first? The set of elements
in a group with order dividing 6 has no canonical indexing. These
are collections defined by a **membership predicate** (`x in S` if
`P x`), not by position.

Phase 2 introduces `Finset` â€” unordered finite collections with
decidable membership â€” to handle exactly this. You'll build finite
sets, prove membership facts, and compute with set operations. The
`Fin` skills you've mastered will appear throughout: `Finset`
membership proofs often reduce to `Fin`-level reasoning.

The most direct bridge: `Finset.range n = {0, 1, ..., n-1}` is
essentially `Fin n` repackaged as an unordered set. Where `Fin n`
gives you *indexed access* (`f i` for `i : Fin n`), `Finset.range n`
gives you *membership* (`k in Finset.range n iff k < n`). Same
elements, different interface â€” and you'll see how the `Fin`-level
reasoning you've mastered transfers directly to the `Finset` world.
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases
DisabledTheorem funext

1â†’import Game.Metadata
     2â†’
     3â†’World "FinsetBasics"
     4â†’Level 18
     5â†’
     6â†’Title "Boss: Finset Fundamentals"
     7â†’
     8â†’Introduction "
     9â†’# Boss: Finset Fundamentals
    10â†’
    11â†’Time to combine everything. You're given a finset `s` that equals
    12â†’`{2, 4, 6}`, and you must prove five facts about it:
    13â†’
    14â†’1. **Membership**: `4 âˆˆ s` â€” peel inserts to find 4
    15â†’2. **Non-membership**: `5 âˆ‰ s` â€” assume membership, derive contradiction
    16â†’3. **Range coverage**: `âˆ€ x âˆˆ s, x âˆˆ Finset.range 7` â€” every element of `s` is in `range 7`
    17â†’4. **Nonempty**: `s.Nonempty` â€” provide a witness element
    18â†’5. **Universal idempotency**: `âˆ€ x âˆˆ s, insert x s = s` â€” combine intro with apply-then-prove
    19â†’
    20â†’Most parts start with `rw [hs]` to substitute the known value of `s`.
    21â†’Part 3 combines multiple skills: substitution, intro, reading membership
    22â†’backward, and range membership. Part 5 combines universally quantified
    23â†’intro with the apply-then-prove pattern from Level 14.
    24â†’"
    25â†’
    26â†’/-- Five fundamental facts about finsets. -/
    27â†’Statement (s : Finset â„•) (hs : s = {2, 4, 6}) :
    28â†’    (4 âˆˆ s) âˆ§ (5 âˆ‰ s) âˆ§ (âˆ€ x âˆˆ s, x âˆˆ Finset.range 7) âˆ§ s.Nonempty âˆ§ (âˆ€ x âˆˆ s, insert x s = s) := by
    29â†’  Hint "The goal is a five-part conjunction. Use `constructor` to split
    30â†’  off the first part."
    31â†’  constructor
    32â†’  -- Part 1: Membership
    33â†’  آ· Hint "Substitute `s`: `rw [hs]`. Then prove membership of `4`
    34â†’    by peeling inserts."
    35â†’    rw [hs]
    36â†’    Hint (hidden := true) "Chain the rewrites:
    37â†’    `rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton]`
    38â†’    gives a three-way disjunction. Then `right; left; rfl`."
    39â†’    rw [Finset.mem_insert]
    40â†’    right
    41â†’    rw [Finset.mem_insert]
    42â†’    left
    43â†’    rfl
    44â†’  Hint "The remaining goal is still a conjunction. Use `constructor`
    45â†’  to split off the next part."
    46â†’  constructor
    47â†’  -- Part 2: Non-membership
    48â†’  آ· Hint "Substitute `s`: `rw [hs]`. Then prove non-membership of `5`."
    49â†’    rw [hs]
    50â†’    Hint (hidden := true) "`intro h`, then
    51â†’    `rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at h`,
    52â†’    then `omega`."
    53â†’    intro h
    54â†’    rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at h
    55â†’    omega
    56â†’  Hint "Another conjunction remains. Use `constructor` again."
    57â†’  constructor
    58â†’  -- Part 3: Range coverage (all elements of s are in range 7)
    59â†’  آ· Hint "Substitute `s`: `rw [hs]`. Then introduce `x` and its
    60â†’    membership hypothesis with `intro x hx`."
    61â†’    rw [hs]
    62â†’    Hint (hidden := true) "After `intro x hx`, unfold `hx` with
    63â†’    `rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at hx`,
    64â†’    then `rw [Finset.mem_range]; omega`."
    65â†’    intro x hx
    66â†’    rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at hx
    67â†’    rw [Finset.mem_range]
    68â†’    omega
    69â†’  Hint "Two parts left. Use `constructor` to split them."
    70â†’  constructor
    71â†’  -- Part 4: Nonempty
    72â†’  آ· Hint "Substitute `s`: `rw [hs]`. Then prove `Nonempty` by
    73â†’    providing a witness with `use`."
    74â†’    rw [hs]
    75â†’    Hint (hidden := true) "`use 2; rw [Finset.mem_insert]; left; rfl`
    76â†’    provides 2 as the witness and proves its membership."
    77â†’    use 2
    78â†’    rw [Finset.mem_insert]
    79â†’    left
    80â†’    rfl
    81â†’  -- Part 5: Universal insert idempotency
    82â†’  آ· Hint "Substitute `s`: `rw [hs]`. Then introduce `x` and its
    83â†’    membership hypothesis with `intro x hx`."
    84â†’    rw [hs]
    85â†’    Hint (hidden := true) "After `intro x hx`, use
    86â†’    `apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem` to reduce to a membership proof,
    87â†’    then `exact hx`."
    88â†’    intro x hx
    89â†’    Hint "Now use the apply-then-prove pattern:
    90â†’    `apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem` reduces the equation to membership."
    91â†’    apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem
    92â†’    Hint (hidden := true) "The goal is membership of `x` in the
    93â†’    concrete set â€” but you already have `{hx}`. Use `exact {hx}`."
    94â†’    exact hx
    95â†’
    96â†’Conclusion "
    97â†’Congratulations â€” you've completed **Finset Basics**!
    98â†’
    99â†’Your finset toolkit:
   100â†’
   101â†’| Move | Tool | Learned in |
   102â†’|---|---|---|
   103â†’| Prove `a âˆˆ insert b s` | `rw [Finset.mem_insert]` then `left`/`right` | Level 1 |
   104â†’| Prove `a âˆˆ {{b}}` (singleton) | `rw [Finset.mem_singleton]` | Level 2 |
   105â†’| Prove `a âˆ‰ âˆ…` | `exact Finset.notMem_empty a` | Level 3 |
   106â†’| Prove `m âˆˆ Finset.range n` | `rw [Finset.mem_range]; omega` | Level 4-8 |
   107â†’| Prove `m âˆ‰ Finset.range n` | `intro h; rw [Finset.mem_range] at h; omega` | Level 5, 11 |
   108â†’| Prove range 0 is empty | non-membership for all elements | Level 6 |
   109â†’| Bridge `Fin n` to `Finset.range n` | `rw [Finset.mem_range]; exact i.isLt` | Level 9-10 |
   110â†’| Prove `a âˆ‰ {{b, c, d}}` | `intro h; rw [mem_insert, ...] at h; omega` | Level 12 |
   111â†’| Unfold `h : x âˆˆ {{a, b, c}}` | `rw [mem_insert, ...] at h` | Level 13 |
   112â†’| Prove `insert a s = s` | `apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem` (apply-then-prove) | Level 14 |
   113â†’| Extract from `s.Nonempty` | `obtain âں¨x, hxâں© := hs` | Level 15 |
   114â†’| Prove `s.Nonempty` | `use a` then prove `a âˆˆ s` | Level 16 |
   115â†’| Close concrete goals | `decide` | Level 17 |
   116â†’
   117â†’**One thing this world can't do yet**: prove that two finsets are
   118â†’**equal**. You showed membership and non-membership, but proving
   119â†’`{{1, 2, 3}} = {{3, 1, 2}}` (same elements, different order) requires
   120â†’showing every element of one is in the other. That's the job of the
   121â†’`ext` tactic, which you'll learn in the FinsetOperations world.
   122â†’
   123â†’**Also missing**: you can prove *what's in* a finset, but not
   124â†’*how many* elements it has. The number of elements is `s.card`
   125â†’(the **cardinality**), and you'll learn to compute it in the
   126â†’Cardinality world.
   127â†’
   128â†’**What's next**: You now know how to build finsets and test
   129â†’membership. Next: what happens when you *combine* finsets? Union
   130â†’(`âˆھ`), intersection (`âˆ©`), and set difference (`\\`) give finsets
   131â†’an algebraic structure, and the `ext` tactic lets you prove two
   132â†’finsets are equal by showing they have the same members.
   133â†’"
   134â†’

import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetBasics"
Level 18

Title "Boss: Finset Fundamentals"

Introduction "
# Boss: Finset Fundamentals

Time to combine everything. You're given a finset `s` that equals
`{2, 4, 6}`, and you must prove five facts about it:

1. **Membership**: `4 âˆˆ s` â€” peel inserts to find 4
2. **Non-membership**: `5 âˆ‰ s` â€” assume membership, derive contradiction
3. **Range coverage**: `âˆ€ x âˆˆ s, x âˆˆ Finset.range 7` â€” every element of `s` is in `range 7`
4. **Nonempty**: `s.Nonempty` â€” provide a witness element
5. **Universal idempotency**: `âˆ€ x âˆˆ s, insert x s = s` â€” combine intro with apply-then-prove

Most parts start with `rw [hs]` to substitute the known value of `s`.
Part 3 combines multiple skills: substitution, intro, reading membership
backward, and range membership. Part 5 combines universally quantified
intro with the apply-then-prove pattern from Level 14.
"

/-- Five fundamental facts about finsets. -/
Statement (s : Finset â„•) (hs : s = {2, 4, 6}) :
    (4 âˆˆ s) âˆ§ (5 âˆ‰ s) âˆ§ (âˆ€ x âˆˆ s, x âˆˆ Finset.range 7) âˆ§ s.Nonempty âˆ§ (âˆ€ x âˆˆ s, insert x s = s) := by
  Hint "The goal is a five-part conjunction. Use `constructor` to split
  off the first part."
  constructor
  -- Part 1: Membership
  آ· Hint "Substitute `s`: `rw [hs]`. Then prove membership of `4`
    by peeling inserts."
    rw [hs]
    Hint (hidden := true) "Chain the rewrites:
    `rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton]`
    gives a three-way disjunction. Then `right; left; rfl`."
    rw [Finset.mem_insert]
    right
    rw [Finset.mem_insert]
    left
    rfl
  Hint "The remaining goal is still a conjunction. Use `constructor`
  to split off the next part."
  constructor
  -- Part 2: Non-membership
  آ· Hint "Substitute `s`: `rw [hs]`. Then prove non-membership of `5`."
    rw [hs]
    Hint (hidden := true) "`intro h`, then
    `rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at h`,
    then `omega`."
    intro h
    rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at h
    omega
  Hint "Another conjunction remains. Use `constructor` again."
  constructor
  -- Part 3: Range coverage (all elements of s are in range 7)
  آ· Hint "Substitute `s`: `rw [hs]`. Then introduce `x` and its
    membership hypothesis with `intro x hx`."
    rw [hs]
    Hint (hidden := true) "After `intro x hx`, unfold `hx` with
    `rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at hx`,
    then `rw [Finset.mem_range]; omega`."
    intro x hx
    rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at hx
    rw [Finset.mem_range]
    omega
  Hint "Two parts left. Use `constructor` to split them."
  constructor
  -- Part 4: Nonempty
  آ· Hint "Substitute `s`: `rw [hs]`. Then prove `Nonempty` by
    providing a witness with `use`."
    rw [hs]
    Hint (hidden := true) "`use 2; rw [Finset.mem_insert]; left; rfl`
    provides 2 as the witness and proves its membership."
    use 2
    rw [Finset.mem_insert]
    left
    rfl
  -- Part 5: Universal insert idempotency
  آ· Hint "Substitute `s`: `rw [hs]`. Then introduce `x` and its
    membership hypothesis with `intro x hx`."
    rw [hs]
    Hint (hidden := true) "After `intro x hx`, use
    `apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem` to reduce to a membership proof,
    then `exact hx`."
    intro x hx
    Hint "Now use the apply-then-prove pattern:
    `apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem` reduces the equation to membership."
    apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem
    Hint (hidden := true) "The goal is membership of `x` in the
    concrete set â€” but you already have `{hx}`. Use `exact {hx}`."
    exact hx

Conclusion "
Congratulations â€” you've completed **Finset Basics**!

Your finset toolkit:

| Move | Tool | Learned in |
|---|---|---|
| Prove `a âˆˆ insert b s` | `rw [Finset.mem_insert]` then `left`/`right` | Level 1 |
| Prove `a âˆˆ {{b}}` (singleton) | `rw [Finset.mem_singleton]` | Level 2 |
| Prove `a âˆ‰ âˆ…` | `exact Finset.notMem_empty a` | Level 3 |
| Prove `m âˆˆ Finset.range n` | `rw [Finset.mem_range]; omega` | Level 4-8 |
| Prove `m âˆ‰ Finset.range n` | `intro h; rw [Finset.mem_range] at h; omega` | Level 5, 11 |
| Prove range 0 is empty | non-membership for all elements | Level 6 |
| Bridge `Fin n` to `Finset.range n` | `rw [Finset.mem_range]; exact i.isLt` | Level 9-10 |
| Prove `a âˆ‰ {{b, c, d}}` | `intro h; rw [mem_insert, ...] at h; omega` | Level 12 |
| Unfold `h : x âˆˆ {{a, b, c}}` | `rw [mem_insert, ...] at h` | Level 13 |
| Prove `insert a s = s` | `apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem` (apply-then-prove) | Level 14 |
| Extract from `s.Nonempty` | `obtain âں¨x, hxâں© := hs` | Level 15 |
| Prove `s.Nonempty` | `use a` then prove `a âˆˆ s` | Level 16 |
| Close concrete goals | `decide` | Level 17 |

**One thing this world can't do yet**: prove that two finsets are
**equal**. You showed membership and non-membership, but proving
`{{1, 2, 3}} = {{3, 1, 2}}` (same elements, different order) requires
showing every element of one is in the other. That's the job of the
`ext` tactic, which you'll learn in the FinsetOperations world.

**Also missing**: you can prove *what's in* a finset, but not
*how many* elements it has. The number of elements is `s.card`
(the **cardinality**), and you'll learn to compute it in the
Cardinality world.

**What's next**: You now know how to build finsets and test
membership. Next: what happens when you *combine* finsets? Union
(`âˆھ`), intersection (`âˆ©`), and set difference (`\\`) give finsets
an algebraic structure, and the `ext` tactic lets you prove two
finsets are equal by showing they have the same members.
"

1â†’import Game.Metadata
     2â†’
     3â†’World "FinsetOperations"
     4â†’Level 25
     5â†’
     6â†’Title "Boss: De Morgan's Second Law"
     7â†’
     8â†’Introduction "
     9â†’# Boss: Set Difference Distributes Over Intersection
    10â†’
    11â†’Time to combine everything from this world. You'll prove a set identity
    12â†’that integrates the unfold-to-logic recipe with set difference and
    13â†’proof by contradiction:
    14â†’
    15â†’$$s \\setminus (t \\cap u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cup (s \\setminus u)$$
    16â†’
    17â†’This is **De Morgan's second law** for sets: removing elements in
    18â†’*both* `t` and `u` is the same as taking elements removed from `t`
    19â†’OR removed from `u`.
    20â†’
    21â†’Compare with Level 17 (De Morgan's first law):
    22â†’$s \\setminus (t \\cup u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cap (s \\setminus u)$.
    23â†’The first law swaps union to intersection; this one swaps intersection
    24â†’to union.
    25â†’
    26â†’After `ext x; simp only [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_union]`,
    27â†’the goal becomes:
    28â†’
    29â†’$$x \\in s \\land \\neg(x \\in t \\land x \\in u) \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; (x \\in s \\land x \\notin t) \\lor (x \\in s \\land x \\notin u)$$
    30â†’
    31â†’The **backward direction** is straightforward: from either side of the
    32â†’disjunction, extract `x \\in s` and derive the negated conjunction by
    33â†’applying the non-membership to a component.
    34â†’
    35â†’The **forward direction** is the challenge. You have `x \\in s` and
    36â†’`\\neg(x \\in t \\land x \\in u)`, but you need to produce `x \\notin t`
    37â†’or `x \\notin u` â€” positive non-membership information from a negated
    38â†’conjunction. This requires **nested** `by_contra` â€” a `by_contra`
    39â†’inside a `by_contra`. The outer `by_contra` assumes the disjunction
    40â†’is false; the inner ones prove each component by contradiction.
    41â†’This nesting pattern is new â€” here's the strategy:
    42â†’
    43â†’1. Assume the goal is false (neither disjunct holds)
    44â†’2. Show that this forces both `x \\in t` and `x \\in u`
    45â†’3. Contradiction with `\\neg(x \\in t \\land x \\in u)`
    46â†’
    47â†’The skill integration:
    48â†’- **ext** (Level 8): convert set equality to logic
    49â†’- **mem_sdiff, mem_inter, mem_union** (Levels 1-3): convert to logic
    50â†’- **simp only** (Level 16): unfold all at once
    51â†’- **by_contra** (Level 18): assume the negation and derive contradiction
    52â†’- **have** (from NNG4): build intermediate results
    53â†’- **constructor, cases, .1, .2, left, right**: standard logic handling
    54â†’"
    55â†’
    56â†’/-- De Morgan's second law for sets. -/
    57â†’Statement (s t u : Finset â„•) : s \ (t âˆ© u) = (s \ t) âˆھ (s \ u) := by
    58â†’  Hint "Apply the unfold-to-logic recipe: `ext x`, then
    59â†’  `simp only [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_union]`."
    60â†’  ext x
    61â†’  simp only [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_union]
    62â†’  Hint "The goal is
    63â†’  `x âˆˆ s âˆ§ آ¬(x âˆˆ t âˆ§ x âˆˆ u) â†” (x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ t) âˆ¨ (x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ u)`.
    64â†’  Use `constructor` to split the biconditional."

    67â†’  آ· Hint "Use `intro h`. Then `h.1 : x âˆˆ s` and
    68â†’    `h.2 : آ¬(x âˆˆ t âˆ§ x âˆˆ u)`. You need to produce
    69â†’    `(x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ t) âˆ¨ (x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ u)`.
    70â†’

    72â†’    but you don't know which one fails. Use `by_contra` to assume

    74â†’    intro h

    76â†’    `h_neg : آ¬((x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ t) âˆ¨ (x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ u))` and changes
    77â†’    the goal to `False`.

    80â†’    contradicting h_neg). Similarly show `x âˆˆ u`. Then
    81â†’    `h.2 (And.intro ht hu)` gives `False`."

    94â†’      by_contra hnt
    95â†’      Hint (hidden := true) "You have `hnt : x âˆ‰ t`. Then
    96â†’      `âں¨h.1, hntâں© : x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ t`, so
    97â†’      `Or.inl âں¨h.1, hntâں© : (x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ t) âˆ¨ (x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ u)`.
    98â†’      This contradicts `h_neg`.

   100â†’      apply h_neg

   103â†’    -- Prove x âˆˆ u

   105â†’      by_contra hnu

   107â†’      apply h_neg

   109â†’      exact âں¨h.1, hnuâں©
   110â†’  -- Backward: (x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ t) âˆ¨ (x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ u) â†’ x âˆˆ s âˆ§ آ¬(x âˆˆ t âˆ§ x âˆˆ u)
   111â†’  آ· Hint "Use `intro h` then `cases h with` to split the disjunction."
   112â†’    intro h
   113â†’    Hint (hidden := true) "In each case, build the conjunction:
   114â†’    `h.1` gives `x âˆˆ s`, and for `آ¬(x âˆˆ t âˆ§ x âˆˆ u)`, use
   115â†’    `intro htu` and apply the non-membership to a component of `htu`."
   116â†’    cases h with
   117â†’    | inl hst =>
   118â†’      Hint (hidden := true) "You have `hst.1 : x âˆˆ s` and `hst.2 : x âˆ‰ t`.
   119â†’      Use `constructor`. First part: `exact hst.1`.
   120â†’      Second part: `intro htu; exact hst.2 htu.1`."
   121â†’      constructor
   122â†’      آ· exact hst.1
   123â†’      آ· intro htu
   124â†’        exact hst.2 htu.1
   125â†’    | inr hsu =>
   126â†’      Hint (hidden := true) "You have `hsu.1 : x âˆˆ s` and `hsu.2 : x âˆ‰ u`.
   127â†’      Use `constructor`. First part: `exact hsu.1`.
   128â†’      Second part: `intro htu; exact hsu.2 htu.2`."
   129â†’      constructor
   130â†’      آ· exact hsu.1
   131â†’      آ· intro htu
   132â†’        exact hsu.2 htu.2
   133â†’
   134â†’Conclusion "
   135â†’Congratulations â€” you've completed **Finset Operations**!
   136â†’
   137â†’You proved De Morgan's second law for sets:
   138â†’$s \\setminus (t \\cap u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cup (s \\setminus u)$.
   139â†’
   140â†’The forward direction required the most sophisticated use of
   141â†’`by_contra` yet: you assumed the *entire disjunction* was false,
   142â†’then showed this forces both `x \\in t` and `x \\in u`, contradicting
   143â†’the negated conjunction. Each component (`x \\in t`, `x \\in u`) was
   144â†’itself proven by contradiction â€” a nested use of `by_contra`.
   145â†’
   146â†’Together with Level 17 (De Morgan's first law), you've proven both
   147â†’De Morgan laws for sets:
   148â†’- $s \\setminus (t \\cup u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cap (s \\setminus u)$
   149â†’- $s \\setminus (t \\cap u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cup (s \\setminus u)$
   150â†’
   151â†’These mirror the propositional De Morgan laws:
   152â†’- $\\neg(P \\lor Q) \\iff \\neg P \\land \\neg Q$
   153â†’- $\\neg(P \\land Q) \\iff \\neg P \\lor \\neg Q$
   154â†’
   155â†’Your finset operations toolkit:
   156â†’
   157â†’| Operation | Membership lemma | Logic form |
   158â†’|---|---|---|
   159â†’| `s âˆھ t` | `Finset.mem_union` | `a âˆˆ s âˆ¨ a âˆˆ t` |
   160â†’| `s âˆ© t` | `Finset.mem_inter` | `a âˆˆ s âˆ§ a âˆˆ t` |
   161â†’| `s \\\\ t` | `Finset.mem_sdiff` | `a âˆˆ s âˆ§ a âˆ‰ t` |
   162â†’| `s.filter p` | `Finset.mem_filter` | `a âˆˆ s âˆ§ p a` |
   163â†’| `s âٹ† t` | `Finset.subset_iff` | `âˆ€ x âˆˆ s, x âˆˆ t` |
   164â†’| `s = t` | `ext` / `Finset.ext_iff` | `âˆ€ x, x âˆˆ s â†” x âˆˆ t` |
   165â†’
   166â†’The proof recipe for any set identity:
   167â†’1. `ext x` â€” reduce to element-wise logic
   168â†’2. `simp only [mem_union, mem_inter, ...]` â€” unfold all operations
   169â†’3. Handle the resulting âˆ§/âˆ¨/آ¬ with `constructor`, `cases`, `left`/`right`
   170â†’4. When stuck on extracting positive from negative: `by_contra`
   171â†’
   172â†’**The bigger picture**: Across this world, you've proved the key
   173â†’axioms of a **distributive lattice with difference**:
   174â†’
   175â†’| Property | Level | Statement |
   176â†’|---|---|---|
   177â†’| Idempotency | L09, L12 | $s \\cap s = s$, $s \\cup s = s$ |
   178â†’| Commutativity | L13, L14 | $s \\cap t = t \\cap s$, $s \\cup t = t \\cup s$ |
   179â†’| Associativity | L15 | $(s \\cup t) \\cup u = s \\cup (t \\cup u)$ |
   180â†’| Identity | L10 | $s \\cup \\emptyset = s$ |
   181â†’| Absorption | L22 | $s \\cup (s \\cap t) = s$ |
   182â†’| Distributivity | L23, L24 | both directions |
   183â†’| De Morgan | L18, L25 | both laws |
   184â†’
   185â†’To get a full **Boolean algebra**, you'd also need a complement
   186â†’operation ($s^c$) and complement laws ($s \\cap s^c = \\emptyset$,
   187â†’$s \\cup s^c = U$). Finsets don't have a natural complement â€”
   188â†’that requires a universal set `U`, which you'll meet in the
   189â†’Cardinality world as `Finset.univ`. For now, set difference
   190â†’(`\\setminus`) plays the role of a relative complement.
   191â†’
   192â†’**What's next**: The next world covers `Finset.image` â€” applying a
   193â†’function to every element of a finset, and the relationship between
   194â†’injectivity and cardinality of images.
   195â†’"

import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetOperations"
Level 25

Title "Boss: De Morgan's Second Law"

Introduction "
# Boss: Set Difference Distributes Over Intersection

Time to combine everything from this world. You'll prove a set identity
that integrates the unfold-to-logic recipe with set difference and
proof by contradiction:

$$s \\setminus (t \\cap u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cup (s \\setminus u)$$

This is **De Morgan's second law** for sets: removing elements in
*both* `t` and `u` is the same as taking elements removed from `t`
OR removed from `u`.

Compare with Level 17 (De Morgan's first law):
$s \\setminus (t \\cup u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cap (s \\setminus u)$.
The first law swaps union to intersection; this one swaps intersection
to union.

After `ext x; simp only [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_union]`,
the goal becomes:

$$x \\in s \\land \\neg(x \\in t \\land x \\in u) \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; (x \\in s \\land x \\notin t) \\lor (x \\in s \\land x \\notin u)$$

The **backward direction** is straightforward: from either side of the
disjunction, extract `x \\in s` and derive the negated conjunction by
applying the non-membership to a component.

The **forward direction** is the challenge. You have `x \\in s` and
`\\neg(x \\in t \\land x \\in u)`, but you need to produce `x \\notin t`
or `x \\notin u` â€” positive non-membership information from a negated
conjunction. This requires **nested** `by_contra` â€” a `by_contra`
inside a `by_contra`. The outer `by_contra` assumes the disjunction
is false; the inner ones prove each component by contradiction.
This nesting pattern is new â€” here's the strategy:

1. Assume the goal is false (neither disjunct holds)
2. Show that this forces both `x \\in t` and `x \\in u`
3. Contradiction with `\\neg(x \\in t \\land x \\in u)`

The skill integration:
- **ext** (Level 8): convert set equality to logic
- **mem_sdiff, mem_inter, mem_union** (Levels 1-3): convert to logic
- **simp only** (Level 16): unfold all at once
- **by_contra** (Level 18): assume the negation and derive contradiction
- **have** (from NNG4): build intermediate results
- **constructor, cases, .1, .2, left, right**: standard logic handling
"

/-- De Morgan's second law for sets. -/
Statement (s t u : Finset â„•) : s \ (t âˆ© u) = (s \ t) âˆھ (s \ u) := by
  Hint "Apply the unfold-to-logic recipe: `ext x`, then
  `simp only [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_union]`."
  ext x
  simp only [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_union]
  Hint "The goal is
  `x âˆˆ s âˆ§ آ¬(x âˆˆ t âˆ§ x âˆˆ u) â†” (x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ t) âˆ¨ (x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ u)`.
  Use `constructor` to split the biconditional."
  constructor
  -- Forward: x âˆˆ s âˆ§ آ¬(x âˆˆ t âˆ§ x âˆˆ u) â†’ (x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ t) âˆ¨ (x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ u)
  آ· Hint "Use `intro h`. Then `h.1 : x âˆˆ s` and
    `h.2 : آ¬(x âˆˆ t âˆ§ x âˆˆ u)`. You need to produce
    `(x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ t) âˆ¨ (x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ u)`.

    The challenge: you know `x âˆˆ t` and `x âˆˆ u` can't BOTH hold,
    but you don't know which one fails. Use `by_contra` to assume
    the whole goal is false, then show both must hold â€” contradiction."
    intro h
    Hint (hidden := true) "Use `by_contra h_neg`. This gives
    `h_neg : آ¬((x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ t) âˆ¨ (x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ u))` and changes
    the goal to `False`.

    Strategy: show `x âˆˆ t` (if not, the left disjunct holds,
    contradicting h_neg). Similarly show `x âˆˆ u`. Then
    `h.2 (And.intro ht hu)` gives `False`."
    by_contra h_neg
    Hint "The goal is `False`. You need to show that `h.2` and `h_neg`
    are contradictory.

    Use `apply h.2` to change the goal to `x âˆˆ t âˆ§ x âˆˆ u` â€” if you
    can build that, `h.2` derives `False`."
    apply h.2
    Hint "The goal is `x âˆˆ t âˆ§ x âˆˆ u`. Use `constructor` to split it."
    constructor
    -- Prove x âˆˆ t
    آ· Hint "The goal is `x âˆˆ t`. Use `by_contra hnt` to assume `x âˆ‰ t`
      and derive a contradiction with `h_neg`."
      by_contra hnt
      Hint (hidden := true) "You have `hnt : x âˆ‰ t`. Then
      `âں¨h.1, hntâں© : x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ t`, so
      `Or.inl âں¨h.1, hntâں© : (x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ t) âˆ¨ (x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ u)`.
      This contradicts `h_neg`.
      Use `apply h_neg; left; exact âں¨h.1, hntâں©`."
      apply h_neg
      left
      exact âں¨h.1, hntâں©
    -- Prove x âˆˆ u
    آ· Hint "Same pattern: `by_contra hnu` then derive a contradiction."
      by_contra hnu
      Hint (hidden := true) "`apply h_neg; right; exact âں¨h.1, hnuâں©`."
      apply h_neg
      right
      exact âں¨h.1, hnuâں©
  -- Backward: (x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ t) âˆ¨ (x âˆˆ s âˆ§ x âˆ‰ u) â†’ x âˆˆ s âˆ§ آ¬(x âˆˆ t âˆ§ x âˆˆ u)
  آ· Hint "Use `intro h` then `cases h with` to split the disjunction."
    intro h
    Hint (hidden := true) "In each case, build the conjunction:
    `h.1` gives `x âˆˆ s`, and for `آ¬(x âˆˆ t âˆ§ x âˆˆ u)`, use
    `intro htu` and apply the non-membership to a component of `htu`."
    cases h with
    | inl hst =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "You have `hst.1 : x âˆˆ s` and `hst.2 : x âˆ‰ t`.
      Use `constructor`. First part: `exact hst.1`.
      Second part: `intro htu; exact hst.2 htu.1`."
      constructor
      آ· exact hst.1
      آ· intro htu
        exact hst.2 htu.1
    | inr hsu =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "You have `hsu.1 : x âˆˆ s` and `hsu.2 : x âˆ‰ u`.
      Use `constructor`. First part: `exact hsu.1`.
      Second part: `intro htu; exact hsu.2 htu.2`."
      constructor
      آ· exact hsu.1
      آ· intro htu
        exact hsu.2 htu.2

Conclusion "
Congratulations â€” you've completed **Finset Operations**!

You proved De Morgan's second law for sets:
$s \\setminus (t \\cap u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cup (s \\setminus u)$.

The forward direction required the most sophisticated use of
`by_contra` yet: you assumed the *entire disjunction* was false,
then showed this forces both `x \\in t` and `x \\in u`, contradicting
the negated conjunction. Each component (`x \\in t`, `x \\in u`) was
itself proven by contradiction â€” a nested use of `by_contra`.

Together with Level 17 (De Morgan's first law), you've proven both
De Morgan laws for sets:
- $s \\setminus (t \\cup u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cap (s \\setminus u)$
- $s \\setminus (t \\cap u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cup (s \\setminus u)$

These mirror the propositional De Morgan laws:
- $\\neg(P \\lor Q) \\iff \\neg P \\land \\neg Q$
- $\\neg(P \\land Q) \\iff \\neg P \\lor \\neg Q$

Your finset operations toolkit:

| Operation | Membership lemma | Logic form |
|---|---|---|
| `s âˆھ t` | `Finset.mem_union` | `a âˆˆ s âˆ¨ a âˆˆ t` |
| `s âˆ© t` | `Finset.mem_inter` | `a âˆˆ s âˆ§ a âˆˆ t` |
| `s \\\\ t` | `Finset.mem_sdiff` | `a âˆˆ s âˆ§ a âˆ‰ t` |
| `s.filter p` | `Finset.mem_filter` | `a âˆˆ s âˆ§ p a` |
| `s âٹ† t` | `Finset.subset_iff` | `âˆ€ x âˆˆ s, x âˆˆ t` |
| `s = t` | `ext` / `Finset.ext_iff` | `âˆ€ x, x âˆˆ s â†” x âˆˆ t` |

The proof recipe for any set identity:
1. `ext x` â€” reduce to element-wise logic
2. `simp only [mem_union, mem_inter, ...]` â€” unfold all operations
3. Handle the resulting âˆ§/âˆ¨/آ¬ with `constructor`, `cases`, `left`/`right`
4. When stuck on extracting positive from negative: `by_contra`

**The bigger picture**: Across this world, you've proved the key
axioms of a **distributive lattice with difference**:

| Property | Level | Statement |
|---|---|---|
| Idempotency | L09, L12 | $s \\cap s = s$, $s \\cup s = s$ |
| Commutativity | L13, L14 | $s \\cap t = t \\cap s$, $s \\cup t = t \\cup s$ |
| Associativity | L15 | $(s \\cup t) \\cup u = s \\cup (t \\cup u)$ |
| Identity | L10 | $s \\cup \\emptyset = s$ |
| Absorption | L22 | $s \\cup (s \\cap t) = s$ |
| Distributivity | L23, L24 | both directions |
| De Morgan | L18, L25 | both laws |

To get a full **Boolean algebra**, you'd also need a complement
operation ($s^c$) and complement laws ($s \\cap s^c = \\emptyset$,
$s \\cup s^c = U$). Finsets don't have a natural complement â€”
that requires a universal set `U`, which you'll meet in the
Cardinality world as `Finset.univ`. For now, set difference
(`\\setminus`) plays the role of a relative complement.

**What's next**: The next world covers `Finset.image` â€” applying a
function to every element of a finset, and the relationship between
injectivity and cardinality of images.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetImage"
Level 23

Title "Boss: Image Membership, Bounds, and Injectivity"

Introduction "
# Boss: Putting It All Together

Consider $f(n) = n + 3$ applied to $S = \\{0, 1, \\ldots, 9\\}$.
This level integrates three core move families from the world.

**Part 1** â€” Forward membership (Levels 1-2):
Prove $12 \\in f(S)$ by providing the witness $9$.

**Part 2** â€” Subset bound via backward extraction (Levels 3-8):
Prove $f(S) \\subseteq \\{0, \\ldots, 12\\}$. Every element
$n + 3$ where $n < 10$ satisfies $n + 3 < 13$.

**Part 3** â€” Cardinality upper bound (Level 12):
The image can only shrink: $|f(S)| \\leq |S|$.

**Part 4** â€” Exact cardinality via injectivity (Levels 13-14):
Prove $|f(S)| = |S|$ by proving that $n \\mapsto n+3$ is injective.

No new moves â€” only moves seeded in Levels 1-21.
"

/-- Forward membership, subset bound, cardinality bounds, and injectivity. -/
Statement :
    (12 : â„•) âˆˆ (Finset.range 10).image (fun n => n + 3) âˆ§
    (Finset.range 10).image (fun n => n + 3) âٹ† Finset.range 13 âˆ§
    ((Finset.range 10).image (fun n => n + 3)).card â‰¤
      (Finset.range 10).card âˆ§
    ((Finset.range 10).image (fun n => n + 3)).card =
      (Finset.range 10).card := by
  Hint "Use `constructor` to split into Part 1 and the remaining parts."
  constructor
  -- Part 1: 12 âˆˆ image (آ· + 3) (range 10)
  آ· Hint "Forward membership: `rw [Finset.mem_image]`, provide the witness.
    Solve: $n + 3 = 12$ gives $n = 9$."
    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    Hint (hidden := true) "Try `use 9`."
    use 9
    Hint "Lean verified `9 + 3 = 12`. Remaining: `9 âˆˆ Finset.range 10`.
    Use `rw [Finset.mem_range]; omega`."
    rw [Finset.mem_range]
    omega
  constructor
  -- Part 2: image (آ· + 3) (range 10) âٹ† range 13
  آ· Hint "Subset goal: introduce an element with `intro x hx`,
    then extract the witness from `hx` and show `x < 13`."
    intro x hx
    Hint "Use backward extraction: `rw [Finset.mem_image] at hx`
    then `obtain âں¨a, ha, hfaâں© := hx`."
    rw [Finset.mem_image] at hx
    obtain âں¨a, ha, hfaâں© := hx
    Hint "You have `ha : a âˆˆ Finset.range 10` and
    `hfa : a + 3 = x`. Rewrite `ha` to get the bound:
    `rw [Finset.mem_range] at ha` gives `a < 10`.
    Then `rw [Finset.mem_range]` on the goal gives `x < 13`.
    Finally `omega` closes it."
    Hint (hidden := true) "Try:
    `rw [Finset.mem_range] at ha`
    `rw [Finset.mem_range]`
    `omega`"
    rw [Finset.mem_range] at ha
    rw [Finset.mem_range]
    omega
  constructor
  -- Part 3: card upper bound
  آ· Hint "The image can only shrink: `Finset.card_image_le` says
    $|f(S)| \\leq |S|$ for any function. Apply it directly."
    Hint (hidden := true) "Try `exact Finset.card_image_le`."
    exact Finset.card_image_le
  -- Part 4: card preserved
  آ· Hint "Now prove the exact count. Apply
    `Finset.card_image_of_injective` â€” this leaves the
    injectivity goal."
    apply Finset.card_image_of_injective
    Hint "Prove `Function.Injective (fun n => n + 3)`.
    Use `show âˆ€ a b : â„•, a + 3 = b + 3 â†’ a = b` to restate
    with the function applied, then `intro a b h; omega`."
    show âˆ€ a b : â„•, a + 3 = b + 3 â†’ a = b
    intro a b h
    omega

Conclusion "
Congratulations â€” you've completed **Finset Image**!

You mastered four core move families:

**1. Forward membership** (witness construction):
- `rw [Finset.mem_image]; use x; constructor`
- Choose `x` by solving `f(x) = y`

**2. Backward extraction** (witness destruction):
- `rw [Finset.mem_image] at h; obtain âں¨x, hx, hfxâں© := h`
- Used for non-membership proofs, subset proofs, and re-use

**3. Cardinality bounds**:
- `Finset.card_image_le`: $|f(S)| \\leq |S|$ (always)
- `Finset.card_image_of_injective`: $|f(S)| = |S|$ (when $f$ is injective)
- `Finset.card_image_iff`: $|f(S)| = |S| \\iff f$ is injective on $S$
- Injectivity proof: `intro a b h; omega`

**4. Image algebra**:
- Image distributes over union: $f(S \\cup T) = f(S) \\cup f(T)$
- Image of intersection: $f(S \\cap T) \\subseteq f(S) \\cap f(T)$
  (equality under injectivity)
- Image of singleton: $f(\\{a\\}) = \\{f(a)\\}$

| Theorem | What it says |
|---|---|
| `Finset.mem_image` | `y âˆˆ s.image f â†” âˆƒ x âˆˆ s, f x = y` |
| `Finset.card_image_le` | `(s.image f).card â‰¤ s.card` |
| `Finset.card_image_of_injective` | injective `f` implies `(s.image f).card = s.card` |
| `Finset.card_image_iff` | `(s.image f).card = s.card â†” Set.InjOn f â†‘s` |

**Injectivity vs. surjectivity**: This world focused on
*injectivity* â€” does $f$ preserve distinctness? The dual question
is *surjectivity* â€” does $f$ cover the target? When does
$s.\\text{image}\\; f = t$ for a target set $t$? Cardinality will
connect the two: counting arguments show that injectivity between
same-size sets *implies* surjectivity. The pigeonhole principle is
a non-surjectivity result in disguise.

**The dual operation**: Everything in this world went forward â€”
given a source set $S$, compute $f(S)$. The dual question goes
backward: given a target set $T$, which elements of the domain
map *into* $T$? That's `Finset.preimage` â€” the finset version of
the preimage. It appears in later worlds when you need to reason
about which inputs produce a desired output.

**What's next**: Problem Set Finset will test these skills on fresh
problems without scaffolding.
"

1â†’import Game.Metadata
     2â†’
     3â†’World "PsetFinset"
     4â†’Level 14
     5â†’
     6â†’Title "Boss: Image Preserves Set Difference"
     7â†’
     8â†’Introduction "
     9â†’# Boss: Image and Set Difference Under Injectivity
    10â†’
    11â†’Given an **injective** function $f$, prove:
    12â†’
    13â†’$$(s \\setminus t).\\text{image}\\; f \\;\\subseteq\\; s.\\text{image}\\; f \\;\\setminus\\; t.\\text{image}\\; f$$
    14â†’
    15â†’If you remove elements of $t$ from $s$ *before* applying $f$,
    16â†’the result is contained in what you'd get by applying $f$ to $s$
    17â†’and then removing the image of $t$.
    18â†’
    19â†’This proof integrates 7+ moves from FinsetOperations and FinsetImage.
    20â†’No new moves â€” only moves you've already used.
    21â†’"
    22â†’
    23â†’/-- Under injectivity, image preserves set difference (as a subset). -/
    24â†’Statement (f : â„• â†’ â„•) (hf : Function.Injective f) (s t : Finset â„•) :
    25â†’    (s \ t).image f âٹ† s.image f \ t.image f := by
    26â†’  rw [Finset.subset_iff]
    27â†’  intro y hy
    28â†’  Hint "Extract the witness from `hy`, unfold the source sdiff,
    29â†’  then build both parts of the target sdiff."
    30â†’  rw [Finset.mem_image] at hy
    31â†’  obtain âں¨a, ha, hfaâں© := hy
    32â†’  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff] at ha
    33â†’  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff]
    34â†’  constructor
    35â†’  -- Part 1: y âˆˆ s.image f
    36â†’  آ· Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [Finset.mem_image]; use a; constructor; exact ha.1; exact hfa`"
    37â†’    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    38â†’    exact âں¨a, ha.1, hfaâں©
    39â†’  -- Part 2: y âˆ‰ t.image f
    40â†’  آ· Hint "The goal is `y âˆ‰ t.image f`. Assume `y âˆˆ t.image f`,
    41â†’    extract a witness `b âˆˆ t`, and use injectivity to derive `a = b`."
    42â†’    Hint (hidden := true) "`intro hyt`, then
    43â†’    `rw [Finset.mem_image] at hyt; obtain âں¨b, hbt, hfbâں© := hyt`."
    44â†’    intro hyt
    45â†’    rw [Finset.mem_image] at hyt
    46â†’    obtain âں¨b, hbt, hfbâں© := hyt
    47â†’    Hint "You need `f a = f b` to apply injectivity. You have
    48â†’    `hfa : f a = y` and `hfb : f b = y` â€” both sides equal `y`.
    49â†’    Use `rw` with both hypotheses to build the equality."
    50â†’    Hint (hidden := true) "`have hfab : f a = f b := by rw [hfa, hfb]`.
    51â†’    Then `have hab : a = b := hf hfab`.
    52â†’    `rw [hab] at ha; exact ha.2 hbt`."
    53â†’    have hfab : f a = f b := by rw [hfa, hfb]
    54â†’    have hab : a = b := hf hfab
    55â†’    rw [hab] at ha
    56â†’    exact ha.2 hbt
    57â†’
    58â†’Conclusion "
    59â†’Congratulations â€” you've completed the **Finset Problem Set**!
    60â†’
    61â†’You proved that injective functions preserve set difference:
    62â†’$f(s \\setminus t) \\subseteq f(s) \\setminus f(t)$ when $f$ is injective.
    63â†’
    64â†’The key insight: injectivity was essential. Without it, two
    65â†’different elements $a \\notin t$ and $b \\in t$ could map to
    66â†’the same $y$, making $y$ appear in $t.\\text{image}\\; f$ even though
    67â†’it came from an element outside $t$. Injectivity forces $a = b$,
    68â†’which creates the contradiction.
    69â†’
    70â†’**Equality, not just subset**: You proved the $\\subseteq$ direction.
    71â†’Under injectivity, the reverse inclusion also holds:
    72â†’$f(s) \\setminus f(t) \\subseteq f(s \\setminus t)$. Together they
    73â†’give **equality**: $f(s \\setminus t) = f(s) \\setminus f(t)$ when
    74â†’$f$ is injective. This is `Finset.image_sdiff` in Mathlib.
    75â†’Injectivity fully 'repairs' the interaction between image and
    76â†’set difference â€” just as it repairs intersection
    77â†’(`Finset.image_inter_of_injOn` from FinsetImage L17).
    78â†’
    79â†’**What's next**: The next world introduces **cardinality** â€”
    80â†’the number of elements in a finset â€” and the fundamental
    81â†’counting identities.
    82â†’"
    83â†’
    84â†’/-- `Finset.image_sdiff s t hf` states that when `f` is injective,
    85â†’`(s \\ t).image f = s.image f \\ t.image f` â€” image distributes
    86â†’over set difference under injectivity. -/
    87â†’TheoremDoc Finset.image_sdiff as "Finset.image_sdiff" in "Finset"
    88â†’
    89â†’/-- `Finset.image_sdiff_of_injOn` is the `InjOn` variant of
    90â†’`Finset.image_sdiff` â€” requires injectivity only on the relevant sets. -/
    91â†’TheoremDoc Finset.image_sdiff_of_injOn as "Finset.image_sdiff_of_injOn" in "Finset"
    92â†’
    93â†’DisabledTactic trivial آ«decideآ» native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto push_neg linarith
    94â†’DisabledTheorem Finset.mem_insert_self Finset.mem_insert_of_mem Finset.mem_image_of_mem Finset.image_subset_image Finset.image_mono Finset.image_union Finset.image_subset_iff Finset.subset_union_left Finset.subset_union_right Finset.mem_union_left Finset.mem_union_right le_sup_left le_sup_right Finset.image_inter Finset.image_inter_of_injOn Finset.image_inter_subset sdiff_sdiff_right_self Finset.image_sdiff Finset.image_sdiff_of_injOn
    95â†’

import Game.Metadata

World "PsetFinset"
Level 14

Title "Boss: Image Preserves Set Difference"

Introduction "
# Boss: Image and Set Difference Under Injectivity

Given an **injective** function $f$, prove:

$$(s \\setminus t).\\text{image}\\; f \\;\\subseteq\\; s.\\text{image}\\; f \\;\\setminus\\; t.\\text{image}\\; f$$

If you remove elements of $t$ from $s$ *before* applying $f$,
the result is contained in what you'd get by applying $f$ to $s$
and then removing the image of $t$.

This proof integrates 7+ moves from FinsetOperations and FinsetImage.
No new moves â€” only moves you've already used.
"

/-- Under injectivity, image preserves set difference (as a subset). -/
Statement (f : â„• â†’ â„•) (hf : Function.Injective f) (s t : Finset â„•) :
    (s \ t).image f âٹ† s.image f \ t.image f := by
  rw [Finset.subset_iff]
  intro y hy
  Hint "Extract the witness from `hy`, unfold the source sdiff,
  then build both parts of the target sdiff."
  rw [Finset.mem_image] at hy
  obtain âں¨a, ha, hfaâں© := hy
  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff] at ha
  rw [Finset.mem_sdiff]
  constructor
  -- Part 1: y âˆˆ s.image f
  آ· Hint (hidden := true) "`rw [Finset.mem_image]; use a; constructor; exact ha.1; exact hfa`"
    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    exact âں¨a, ha.1, hfaâں©
  -- Part 2: y âˆ‰ t.image f
  آ· Hint "The goal is `y âˆ‰ t.image f`. Assume `y âˆˆ t.image f`,
    extract a witness `b âˆˆ t`, and use injectivity to derive `a = b`."
    Hint (hidden := true) "`intro hyt`, then
    `rw [Finset.mem_image] at hyt; obtain âں¨b, hbt, hfbâں© := hyt`."
    intro hyt
    rw [Finset.mem_image] at hyt
    obtain âں¨b, hbt, hfbâں© := hyt
    Hint "You need `f a = f b` to apply injectivity. You have
    `hfa : f a = y` and `hfb : f b = y` â€” both sides equal `y`.
    Use `rw` with both hypotheses to build the equality."
    Hint (hidden := true) "`have hfab : f a = f b := by rw [hfa, hfb]`.
    Then `have hab : a = b := hf hfab`.
    `rw [hab] at ha; exact ha.2 hbt`."
    have hfab : f a = f b := by rw [hfa, hfb]
    have hab : a = b := hf hfab
    rw [hab] at ha
    exact ha.2 hbt

Conclusion "
Congratulations â€” you've completed the **Finset Problem Set**!

You proved that injective functions preserve set difference:
$f(s \\setminus t) \\subseteq f(s) \\setminus f(t)$ when $f$ is injective.

The key insight: injectivity was essential. Without it, two
different elements $a \\notin t$ and $b \\in t$ could map to
the same $y$, making $y$ appear in $t.\\text{image}\\; f$ even though
it came from an element outside $t$. Injectivity forces $a = b$,
which creates the contradiction.

**Equality, not just subset**: You proved the $\\subseteq$ direction.
Under injectivity, the reverse inclusion also holds:
$f(s) \\setminus f(t) \\subseteq f(s \\setminus t)$. Together they
give **equality**: $f(s \\setminus t) = f(s) \\setminus f(t)$ when
$f$ is injective. This is `Finset.image_sdiff` in Mathlib.
Injectivity fully 'repairs' the interaction between image and
set difference â€” just as it repairs intersection
(`Finset.image_inter_of_injOn` from FinsetImage L17).

**What's next**: The next world introduces **cardinality** â€”
the number of elements in a finset â€” and the fundamental
counting identities.
"

/-- `Finset.image_sdiff s t hf` states that when `f` is injective,
`(s \\ t).image f = s.image f \\ t.image f` â€” image distributes
over set difference under injectivity. -/
TheoremDoc Finset.image_sdiff as "Finset.image_sdiff" in "Finset"

/-- `Finset.image_sdiff_of_injOn` is the `InjOn` variant of
`Finset.image_sdiff` â€” requires injectivity only on the relevant sets. -/
TheoremDoc Finset.image_sdiff_of_injOn as "Finset.image_sdiff_of_injOn" in "Finset"

import Game.Metadata

World "Cardinality"
Level 24

Title "Boss: Cardinality Chain"

Introduction "
# Boss: Chaining the Card Lemmas

You have a full cardinality toolkit. This boss tests whether you can
chain multiple card lemmas together in a single proof.

**The setup**: You have finsets `s` and `t` with known sizes and overlap.
A subset `u` of the set difference `s \\ t` is given. An element `a`
is known to belong to `t`.

**Your task**: Prove a single inequality that requires FOUR cardinality
facts working together:

1. **Complement counting** (`card_sdiff_add_card_inter`): figure out
   `|(s \\ t)|` from `|s|` and `|s \\cap t|`
2. **Monotonicity** (`card_le_card`): bound `|u|` since `u \\subseteq s \\ t`
3. **Erase** (`card_erase_of_mem`): figure out `|t.\\text{erase}\\;a|`
   since `a \\in t`
4. **Inclusion-exclusion** (`card_union_add_card_inter`): figure out
   `|s \\cup t|` from the individual sizes and overlap

Bring all four facts into context with `have`, then let `omega`
combine them.
"

/-- Chain four card lemmas to prove an inequality. -/
Statement (s t u : Finset â„•) (a : â„•) (ha : a âˆˆ t)
    (hs : s.card = 10) (ht : t.card = 6) (hi : (s âˆ© t).card = 4)
    (hsub : u âٹ† s \ t) :
    u.card + (t.erase a).card â‰¤ (s âˆھ t).card := by
  Hint "You need four cardinality facts. Start with complement counting:
  `have h1 := Finset.card_sdiff_add_card_inter s t`"
  Hint (hidden := true) "The four `have` statements you need:
  `have h1 := Finset.card_sdiff_add_card_inter s t`
  `have h2 := Finset.card_le_card hsub`
  `have h3 := Finset.card_erase_of_mem ha`
  `have h4 := Finset.card_union_add_card_inter s t`
  Then `omega`."
  have h1 := Finset.card_sdiff_add_card_inter s t
  Hint "Good. `h1 : (s \\ t).card + (s \\cap t).card = s.card`.
  With `hs` and `hi`, this gives `(s \\ t).card = 6`.

  Now bound `u.card` using monotonicity."
  have h2 := Finset.card_le_card hsub
  Hint "Good. `h2 : u.card \\leq (s \\ t).card`.

  Now compute the erase cardinality."
  have h3 := Finset.card_erase_of_mem ha
  Hint "Good. `h3 : (t.erase a).card = t.card - 1`.

  Finally, apply inclusion-exclusion for the union."
  have h4 := Finset.card_union_add_card_inter s t
  Hint "`omega` can now combine all four facts with the given sizes."
  omega

Conclusion "
You chained four card lemmas in a single proof:

| Step | Lemma | What it gave |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `card_sdiff_add_card_inter` | $|s \\setminus t| + 4 = 10$, so $|s \\setminus t| = 6$ |
| 2 | `card_le_card` | $|u| \\leq |s \\setminus t| = 6$ |
| 3 | `card_erase_of_mem` | $|t.\\text{erase}\\;a| = 6 - 1 = 5$ |
| 4 | `card_union_add_card_inter` | $|s \\cup t| + 4 = 16$, so $|s \\cup t| = 12$ |

Then `omega` combined: $|u| + 5 \\leq 6 + 5 = 11 \\leq 12$. âœ“

This is the general cardinality proof strategy: **collect card facts
into the context, then let omega combine them**. The mathematical
thinking goes into choosing which lemmas to apply and in what order;
the arithmetic is mechanical.

Your complete cardinality toolkit:

| Lemma | What it says |
|---|---|
| `card_empty` | $|\\emptyset| = 0$ |
| `card_singleton` | $|\\{a\\}| = 1$ |
| `card_insert_of_notMem` | inserting a new element adds 1 |
| `card_range` | $|\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\}| = n$ |
| `card_erase_of_mem` | erasing an existing element subtracts 1 |
| `card_le_card` | subsets have $\\leq$ cardinality |
| `card_union_add_card_inter` | inclusion-exclusion |
| `card_union_of_disjoint` | $|s \\cup t| = |s| + |t|$ when disjoint |
| `card_sdiff_add_card_inter` | complement counting |
| `card_product` | $|s \\times^s t| = |s| \\cdot |t|$ |
| `card_pos` | $0 < |s| \\leftrightarrow s.\\text{Nonempty}$ |
| `card_eq_zero` | $|s| = 0 \\leftrightarrow s = \\emptyset$ |
| `filter_subset` | $s.\\text{filter}\\;p \\subseteq s$ (combine with `card_le_card`) |
| `card_univ` | $|\\text{univ}| = \\text{Fintype.card}\\;\\alpha$ |
| `card_fin` | $\\text{Fintype.card}\\;(\\text{Fin}\\;n) = n$ |
| `subset_univ` | every finset is a subset of univ |
| `eq_of_subset_of_card_le` | subset + same size = equal |

**What's next**: You've learned to count *how many* elements a finset
has. The natural next question: what if you want to *add up a value*
at each element? That's the big operator $\\sum_{x \\in s} f(x)$,
written `\\sum x \\in s, f x` in Lean. Big operators turn counting
into computation â€” and they're built on exactly the card lemma
pattern you just mastered.

The course continues with:
- The **Abstraction Ladder** â€” the List -> Multiset -> Finset structure
- **Fintype** â€” counting elements of composite types
- **Big operators** â€” sums and products over finsets
- **Combinatorics** â€” binomial coefficients, powerset counting, and the binomial theorem

Phases 4-7 are under construction. Phases 1-3 (Fin, Finset, Cardinality)
are complete.
"

TheoremTab "Card"

1â†’import Game.Metadata
     2â†’
     3â†’World "AbstractionLadder"
     4â†’Level 23
     5â†’
     6â†’Title "Boss: Across the Ladder"
     7â†’
     8â†’Introduction "
     9â†’# Boss: Connecting All Three Layers
    10â†’
    11â†’This boss tests your ability to move across the abstraction ladder
    12â†’in a single proof.
    13â†’
    14â†’**The setup**: You have two lists `lâ‚پ` and `lâ‚‚` that are permutations
    15â†’of each other. You know `lâ‚پ` has no duplicates.
    16â†’
    17â†’**Your task**: Prove that `lâ‚‚.toFinset.card = lâ‚پ.length`.
    18â†’
    19â†’This requires four facts working together:
    20â†’
    21â†’1. **Permutations preserve nodup** (`List.Perm.nodup_iff`):
    22â†’   Since `lâ‚پ` has no duplicates and `lâ‚پ ~ lâ‚‚`, then `lâ‚‚` has no
    23â†’   duplicates either.
    24â†’
    25â†’2. **Nodup card = length** (`List.toFinset_card_of_nodup`):
    26â†’   Since `lâ‚‚` has no duplicates, `lâ‚‚.toFinset.card = lâ‚‚.length`.
    27â†’
    28â†’3. **Permutations preserve length** (`List.Perm.length_eq`):
    29â†’   Since `lâ‚پ ~ lâ‚‚`, they have the same length.
    30â†’
    31â†’4. **Chain the equalities**: Combine steps 2 and 3.
    32â†’
    33â†’Use `have` statements to build up intermediate facts.
    34â†’"
    35â†’
    36â†’/-- Permuted nodup lists give finsets with card equal to the original length. -/
    37â†’Statement (lâ‚پ lâ‚‚ : List â„•) (hp : lâ‚پ.Perm lâ‚‚) (hnd : lâ‚پ.Nodup) :
    38â†’    lâ‚‚.toFinset.card = lâ‚پ.length := by
    39â†’  Hint "Start by showing `lâ‚‚` also has no duplicates.
    40â†’  `List.Perm.nodup_iff` gives `lâ‚پ.Perm lâ‚‚ â†’ (lâ‚پ.Nodup â†” lâ‚‚.Nodup)`.
    41â†’  Use it with `hp` and `hnd`."
    42â†’  Hint (hidden := true) "Try:
    43â†’  `have hnd2 : lâ‚‚.Nodup := hp.nodup_iff.mp hnd`"
    44â†’  have hnd2 : lâ‚‚.Nodup := hp.nodup_iff.mp hnd
    45â†’  Hint "Good. Now use `toFinset_card_of_nodup` to convert the finset
    46â†’  card to a list length."
    47â†’  Hint (hidden := true) "Try:
    48â†’  `rw [List.toFinset_card_of_nodup hnd2]`"
    49â†’  rw [List.toFinset_card_of_nodup hnd2]
    50â†’  Hint "The goal is now `lâ‚‚.length = lâ‚پ.length`. The equation is
    51â†’  backwards: `hp.length_eq` gives `lâ‚پ.length = lâ‚‚.length`. Two options:
    52â†’  - Use `rw [â†گ hp.length_eq]` to rewrite right-to-left (as in L08)
    53â†’  - Use `exact hp.length_eq.symm` to flip the equation directly"
    54â†’  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [â†گ hp.length_eq]` to rewrite the goal,
    55â†’  or `exact hp.length_eq.symm` to close it directly."
    56â†’  Branch
    57â†’    rw [â†گ hp.length_eq]
    58â†’  exact hp.length_eq.symm
    59â†’
    60â†’Conclusion "
    61â†’You traversed the full abstraction ladder in one proof:
    62â†’
    63â†’| Step | Tool | What it did |
    64â†’|---|---|---|
    65â†’| 1 | `Perm.nodup_iff` | Transferred nodup across the permutation |
    66â†’| 2 | `toFinset_card_of_nodup` | Converted finset card to list length |
    67â†’| 3 | `Perm.length_eq` | Equated the two list lengths |
    68â†’
    69â†’This proof touched all three layers:
    70â†’- **List layer**: `Nodup`, `length`, `Perm`
    71â†’- **Multiset layer**: The permutation quotient (implicit in `toFinset`)
    72â†’- **Finset layer**: `.card` of the resulting finset
    73â†’
    74â†’The abstraction ladder is not just a theoretical construction â€” it's
    75â†’a practical proof tool. When you need to reason about finset
    76â†’cardinality, you can descend to the list level, use list-level tools
    77â†’(permutations, nodup, length), and bring the results back up.
    78â†’
    79â†’**Your toolkit from this world**:
    80â†’
    81â†’| Concept | Key lemma |
    82â†’|---|---|
    83â†’| List length | `length_cons`, `length_append` |
    84â†’| List membership | `mem_cons` |
    85â†’| Permutation | `Perm.swap`, `Perm.cons`, `Perm.trans` |
    86â†’| Perm â†’ equal length | `Perm.length_eq` |
    87â†’| Perm â†’ equal finsets | `toFinset_eq_of_perm` |
    88â†’| Nodup (no duplicates) | `nodup_cons`, `nodup_nil` |
    89â†’| Perm preserves nodup | `Perm.nodup_iff` |
    90â†’| List â†’ Multiset | `Multiset.coe_eq_coe` |
    91â†’| Multiset card | `Multiset.card_cons`, `coe_card` |
    92â†’| List â†’ Finset | `List.toFinset`, `mem_toFinset` |
    93â†’| Nodup card = length | `toFinset_card_of_nodup` |
    94â†’
    95â†’**A note on `Finset.map`**: You may encounter `Finset.map` in Mathlib.
    96â†’It works like `Finset.image` but bundles an injectivity proof with the
    97â†’function (using `Function.Embedding`). Since `map` knows the function
    98â†’is injective, it can skip deduplication â€” making it more efficient
    99â†’internally. The relationship is `Finset.map_eq_image`: for injective
   100â†’functions, `map` and `image` give the same result.
   101â†’
   102â†’**What's next**: The `Fintype` world uses this abstraction ladder to
   103â†’count elements of composite types like products and function spaces.
   104â†’"
   105â†’
   106â†’TheoremTab "List"
   107â†’
   108â†’DisabledTactic trivial آ«decideآ» native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto linarith
   109â†’DisabledTheorem List.perm_cons_erase List.Perm.decidable
   110â†’

import Game.Metadata

World "AbstractionLadder"
Level 23

Title "Boss: Across the Ladder"

Introduction "
# Boss: Connecting All Three Layers

This boss tests your ability to move across the abstraction ladder
in a single proof.

**The setup**: You have two lists `lâ‚پ` and `lâ‚‚` that are permutations
of each other. You know `lâ‚پ` has no duplicates.

**Your task**: Prove that `lâ‚‚.toFinset.card = lâ‚پ.length`.

This requires four facts working together:

1. **Permutations preserve nodup** (`List.Perm.nodup_iff`):
   Since `lâ‚پ` has no duplicates and `lâ‚پ ~ lâ‚‚`, then `lâ‚‚` has no
   duplicates either.

2. **Nodup card = length** (`List.toFinset_card_of_nodup`):
   Since `lâ‚‚` has no duplicates, `lâ‚‚.toFinset.card = lâ‚‚.length`.

3. **Permutations preserve length** (`List.Perm.length_eq`):
   Since `lâ‚پ ~ lâ‚‚`, they have the same length.

4. **Chain the equalities**: Combine steps 2 and 3.

Use `have` statements to build up intermediate facts.
"

/-- Permuted nodup lists give finsets with card equal to the original length. -/
Statement (lâ‚پ lâ‚‚ : List â„•) (hp : lâ‚پ.Perm lâ‚‚) (hnd : lâ‚پ.Nodup) :
    lâ‚‚.toFinset.card = lâ‚پ.length := by
  Hint "Start by showing `lâ‚‚` also has no duplicates.
  `List.Perm.nodup_iff` gives `lâ‚پ.Perm lâ‚‚ â†’ (lâ‚پ.Nodup â†” lâ‚‚.Nodup)`.
  Use it with `hp` and `hnd`."
  Hint (hidden := true) "Try:
  `have hnd2 : lâ‚‚.Nodup := hp.nodup_iff.mp hnd`"
  have hnd2 : lâ‚‚.Nodup := hp.nodup_iff.mp hnd
  Hint "Good. Now use `toFinset_card_of_nodup` to convert the finset
  card to a list length."
  Hint (hidden := true) "Try:
  `rw [List.toFinset_card_of_nodup hnd2]`"
  rw [List.toFinset_card_of_nodup hnd2]
  Hint "The goal is now `lâ‚‚.length = lâ‚پ.length`. The equation is
  backwards: `hp.length_eq` gives `lâ‚پ.length = lâ‚‚.length`. Two options:
  - Use `rw [â†گ hp.length_eq]` to rewrite right-to-left (as in L08)
  - Use `exact hp.length_eq.symm` to flip the equation directly"
  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [â†گ hp.length_eq]` to rewrite the goal,
  or `exact hp.length_eq.symm` to close it directly."
  Branch
    rw [â†گ hp.length_eq]
  exact hp.length_eq.symm

Conclusion "
You traversed the full abstraction ladder in one proof:

| Step | Tool | What it did |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `Perm.nodup_iff` | Transferred nodup across the permutation |
| 2 | `toFinset_card_of_nodup` | Converted finset card to list length |
| 3 | `Perm.length_eq` | Equated the two list lengths |

This proof touched all three layers:
- **List layer**: `Nodup`, `length`, `Perm`
- **Multiset layer**: The permutation quotient (implicit in `toFinset`)
- **Finset layer**: `.card` of the resulting finset

The abstraction ladder is not just a theoretical construction â€” it's
a practical proof tool. When you need to reason about finset
cardinality, you can descend to the list level, use list-level tools
(permutations, nodup, length), and bring the results back up.

**Your toolkit from this world**:

| Concept | Key lemma |
|---|---|
| List length | `length_cons`, `length_append` |
| List membership | `mem_cons` |
| Permutation | `Perm.swap`, `Perm.cons`, `Perm.trans` |
| Perm â†’ equal length | `Perm.length_eq` |
| Perm â†’ equal finsets | `toFinset_eq_of_perm` |
| Nodup (no duplicates) | `nodup_cons`, `nodup_nil` |
| Perm preserves nodup | `Perm.nodup_iff` |
| List â†’ Multiset | `Multiset.coe_eq_coe` |
| Multiset card | `Multiset.card_cons`, `coe_card` |
| List â†’ Finset | `List.toFinset`, `mem_toFinset` |
| Nodup card = length | `toFinset_card_of_nodup` |

**A note on `Finset.map`**: You may encounter `Finset.map` in Mathlib.
It works like `Finset.image` but bundles an injectivity proof with the
function (using `Function.Embedding`). Since `map` knows the function
is injective, it can skip deduplication â€” making it more efficient
internally. The relationship is `Finset.map_eq_image`: for injective
functions, `map` and `image` give the same result.

**What's next**: The `Fintype` world uses this abstraction ladder to
count elements of composite types like products and function spaces.
"

TheoremTab "List"

import Game.Metadata

World "Fintype"
Level 12

Title "Boss: Composite Cardinality"

Introduction "
# Boss: How Many Functions?

Time to put everything together. You have six card_* lemmas:

| Lemma | What it decomposes |
|---|---|
| `Fintype.card_fin` | `card (Fin n) = n` |
| `Fintype.card_bool` | `card Bool = 2` |
| `Fintype.card_prod` | `card (خ± أ— خ²) = card خ± * card خ²` |
| `Fintype.card_sum` | `card (خ± âٹ• خ²) = card خ± + card خ²` |
| `Fintype.card_fun` | `card (خ± â†’ خ²) = card خ² ^ card خ±` |
| `Fintype.card_congr e` | `خ± â‰ƒ خ² â†’ card خ± = card خ²` |

**Your task**: Given an abstract type `خ±` that is equivalent to
`Fin 2 أ— Bool`, compute the number of functions from `خ±` to
`Fin 2 âٹ• Fin 3`.

Think about it mathematically first:
- `خ± â‰ƒ Fin 2 أ— Bool`, so `card خ± = card (Fin 2 أ— Bool) = 2 * 2 = 4`
- The codomain `Fin 2 âٹ• Fin 3` has `2 + 3 = 5` elements
- The number of functions is `5 ^ 4 = 625`

You'll need ALL six lemmas (plus `omega` for the final arithmetic).
"

/-- The number of functions from خ± to Fin 2 âٹ• Fin 3 is 625,
    given that خ± â‰ƒ Fin 2 أ— Bool. -/
Statement (خ± : Type*) [DecidableEq خ±] [Fintype خ±] (e : خ± â‰ƒ Fin 2 أ— Bool) :
    Fintype.card (خ± â†’ Fin 2 âٹ• Fin 3) = 625 := by
  Hint "Start by decomposing the function type with `rw [Fintype.card_fun]`."
  rw [Fintype.card_fun]
  Hint "Now use the equivalence `e` to resolve `Fintype.card خ±`.
  Try `rw [Fintype.card_congr e]`."
  rw [Fintype.card_congr e]
  Hint "Decompose the domain with `rw [Fintype.card_prod]` and the
  codomain with `rw [Fintype.card_sum]`."
  rw [Fintype.card_prod, Fintype.card_sum]
  Hint "Evaluate the individual type cardinalities with `card_fin` and
  `card_bool`."
  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [Fintype.card_fin, Fintype.card_fin, Fintype.card_bool]`."
  rw [Fintype.card_fin, Fintype.card_fin, Fintype.card_bool]
  Hint "The goal is now `(2 + 3) ^ (2 * 2) = 625`, i.e., `5 ^ 4 = 625`.
  Use `omega` for the arithmetic."
  omega

Conclusion "
You computed the cardinality of a composite function type using ALL
six card_* lemmas:

$$|\\alpha \\to \\text{Fin}\\;2 \\oplus \\text{Fin}\\;3| = 5^4 = 625$$

The proof decomposed from the outside in:

| Step | Lemma | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `card_fun` | split into `card (Fin 2 âٹ• Fin 3) ^ card خ±` |
| 2 | `card_congr e` | resolve `card خ±` to `card (Fin 2 أ— Bool)` |
| 3 | `card_prod` | decompose domain `card (Fin 2) * card Bool` |
| 4 | `card_sum` | decompose codomain `card (Fin 2) + card (Fin 3)` |
| 5 | `card_fin`, `card_bool` | evaluate to `(2 + 3) ^ (2 * 2)` |
| 6 | `omega` | `5 ^ 4 = 625` |

## Your Fintype Toolkit

| Lemma | Formula |
|---|---|
| `card_fin n` | `card (Fin n) = n` |
| `card_bool` | `card Bool = 2` |
| `card_prod` | `card (خ± أ— خ²) = card خ± * card خ²` |
| `card_sum` | `card (خ± âٹ• خ²) = card خ± + card خ²` |
| `card_fun` | `card (خ± â†’ خ²) = card خ² ^ card خ±` |
| `card_congr e` | `خ± â‰ƒ خ² â†’ card خ± = card خ²` |
| `card_subtype_compl` | `card negP = card خ± - card P` |

These seven lemmas can compute the cardinality of any type built from
finite building blocks using products, sums, function types,
equivalences, and subtypes.
"

TheoremTab "Fintype"
