1â†’import Game.Metadata
     2â†’
     3â†’World "MeetFin"
     4â†’Level 1
     5â†’
     6â†’Title "What is Fin n?"
     7â†’
     8â†’Introduction "
     9â†’# What is `Fin n`?
    10â†’
    11â†’In mathematics, we often work with finite index sets like
    12â†’$\\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\\}$ â€” the first $n$ natural numbers.
    13â†’
    14â†’In Lean 4, this is captured by the type `Fin n`. An element of `Fin n`
    15â†’is a natural number `k` **together with a proof** that `k < n`.
    16â†’
    17â†’More precisely:
    18â†’$$\\texttt{Fin } n = \\{ k : \\mathbb{N} \\mid k < n \\}$$
    19â†’
    20â†’**Why not just use `â„•`?** If you index a length-5 vector with a plain
    21â†’natural number, nothing stops you from using `7` â€” the type checker
    22â†’can't tell it's out of bounds. With `Fin 5`, the bound is part of the
    23â†’type: you literally *cannot construct* an element unless you prove it's
    24â†’in range. The type system enforces the constraint for you.
    25â†’
    26â†’You might wonder why `Fin n` starts at 0 rather than 1.
    27â†’Zero-indexing means the values of `Fin n` are exactly the natural
    28â†’numbers less than `n`, which aligns with how Lean indexes lists and
    29â†’arrays. It also means `Fin n` has a natural 'zero' element when
    30â†’`n > 0`.
    31â†’
    32â†’This is an example of a *subtype* â€” a type carved out of a larger type
    33â†’by a predicate. `Fin n` is the subtype `{ k : â„• // k < n }`.
    34â†’
    35â†’To construct an element of `Fin n`, use the **anonymous constructor**
    36â†’`âں¨k, proofâں©`, where:
    37â†’- `k` is the natural number
    38â†’- `proof` shows `k < n`
    39â†’
    40â†’For the proof part, the `omega` tactic handles routine arithmetic
    41â†’goals like `3 < 5`.
    42â†’
    43â†’**Your task**: Construct any element of `Fin 5`. There are five to
    44â†’choose from: $0, 1, 2, 3, 4$.
    45â†’
    46â†’Try `exact âں¨3, by omegaâں©`.
    47â†’
    48â†’*Notation*: type `âں¨` as `\\langle` and `âں©` as `\\rangle`.
    49â†’
    50â†’**Important**: Lean happens to accept `exact 3` for this level
    51â†’because it can figure out the bound automatically when it's a
    52â†’concrete number like `5`. But this shortcut will **stop working**
    53â†’starting at Level 12, when the bound becomes a variable `n`.
    54â†’Practice `âں¨k, by omegaâں©` now â€” it's the pattern that always works.
    55â†’"
    56â†’
    57â†’-- INTENTIONAL DESIGN EXCEPTION (P0-1 accepted):
    58â†’-- The goal type `Fin 5` allows `exact 0`, `exact 3`, etc. via Lean's
    59â†’-- OfNat coercion. This is deliberately accepted as an onboarding choice:
    60â†’--
    61â†’-- Rationale: This is the very first level of the entire course. Forcing
    62â†’-- the constructor pattern (e.g. via a variable bound or subtype wrapper)
    63â†’-- would introduce additional complexity that overwhelms true beginners.
    64â†’-- The introduction explicitly teaches `âں¨k, by omegaâں©` and asks the
    65â†’-- learner to use it. The conclusion explicitly warns that the shortcut
    66â†’-- (`exact 3`) only works for concrete bounds and that the explicit
    67â†’-- constructor becomes mandatory from Level 12 onward.
    68â†’--
    69â†’-- The pedagogical contract: the level TEACHES the pattern without
    70â†’-- FORCING it. The learner who follows instructions learns the right
    71â†’-- skill; the learner who discovers the shortcut gets a preview of
    72â†’-- Lean's coercion system, which is itself educational.
    73â†’--
    74â†’-- Alternatives considered and rejected:
    75â†’-- - Variable bound (`Fin (n+1)`): `exact 0` still works via Zero instance
    76â†’-- - Subtype wrapper (`{ i : Fin 5 // i.val = 3 }`): too complex for L01
    77â†’-- - Disabling OfNat: not possible via DisabledTheorem
    78â†’
    79â†’/-- Constructs a specific element of `Fin 5`. -/
    80â†’Statement : Fin 5 := by
    81â†’  Hint "Use the anonymous constructor `âں¨k, by omegaâں©` where `k` is any
    82â†’  natural number less than 5. For example, `exact âں¨3, by omegaâں©`."
    83â†’  exact âں¨3, by omegaâں©
    84â†’
    85â†’Conclusion "
    86â†’Well done! You just constructed the element $3$ as a term of type
    87â†’`Fin 5`.
    88â†’
    89â†’The key pattern: to build a `Fin n`, you provide **two things** â€” a
    90â†’number and a proof that it's in range. The `âں¨k, by omegaâں©` idiom
    91â†’will appear throughout this world.
    92â†’
    93â†’In ordinary mathematics, saying *let $x = 3 \\in \\{0, \\ldots, 4\\}$*
    94â†’is informal. In Lean, you must **prove** the element is in range.
    95â†’That's what the second component does.
    96â†’
    97â†’**Design note**: `exact 3` also works here â€” Lean can automatically
    98â†’coerce a numeric literal into `Fin 5` when the bound is known.
    99â†’We intentionally allow this shortcut in this first level so you can
   100â†’focus on understanding the *structure* of `Fin` without getting stuck
   101â†’on syntax. But practice the `âں¨k, by omegaâں©` pattern now, because
   102â†’starting in Level 12, the type bound will be a variable `n` â€” not a
   103â†’concrete number â€” and the automatic coercion won't work. The explicit
   104â†’constructor is the general-purpose tool.
   105â†’"
   106â†’
   107â†’/-- `omega` closes goals involving linear arithmetic over `â„•` and `â„¤`.
   108â†’It handles equalities (`=`), inequalities (`<`, `â‰¤`, `â‰¥`, `>`),
   109â†’and negated equalities (`â‰ `).
   110â†’
   111â†’## Syntax
   112â†’```
   113â†’omega
   114â†’```
   115â†’
   116â†’## When to use it
   117â†’- When the goal follows from linear arithmetic: `3 < 5`, `n + 1 â‰¤ m`
   118â†’- When hypotheses contradict each other: if you have `h1 : x = 5` and
   119â†’  `h2 : x < 5`, omega sees the contradiction and closes any goal
   120â†’- When multiple hypotheses combine: omega reasons across ALL hypotheses
   121â†’  simultaneously, not just one at a time
   122â†’
   123â†’## Examples
   124â†’```
   125â†’-- Goal: 3 < 5
   126â†’omega
   127â†’
   128â†’-- h1 : x = 5, h2 : x < 5 â†’ closes any goal (contradiction)
   129â†’omega
   130â†’
   131â†’-- h1 : a â‰¤ b, h2 : b < c â†’ Goal: a < c
   132â†’omega
   133â†’```
   134â†’
   135â†’## Warning
   136â†’`omega` only handles *linear* arithmetic. It cannot solve goals
   137â†’involving multiplication of variables (like `n * m < n * n + 1`).
   138â†’-/
   139â†’TacticDoc omega
   140â†’
   141â†’/-- `Fin n` is the type of natural numbers strictly less than `n`.
   142â†’
   143â†’An element of `Fin n` consists of:
   144â†’- `val : â„•` â€” the underlying natural number
   145â†’- `isLt : val < n` â€” a proof that the number is in range
   146â†’
   147â†’`Fin n` is the subtype `{ k : â„• // k < n }`.
   148â†’
   149â†’## Construction
   150â†’Use the anonymous constructor: `âں¨k, proofâں©`
   151â†’
   152â†’## Example
   153â†’`âں¨3, by omegaâں© : Fin 5` â€” the number 3 with a proof that `3 < 5`.
   154â†’
   155â†’## Warning
   156â†’`Fin n` has elements `0, 1, ..., n-1` (NOT `1, ..., n`).
   157â†’`Fin 0` is **empty** â€” there is no natural number less than 0.
   158â†’-/
   159â†’DefinitionDoc Fin as "Fin"
   160â†’
   161â†’/-- `Fin.mk val isLt` constructs an element of `Fin n` from a natural
   162â†’number `val` and a proof `isLt : val < n`.
   163â†’
   164â†’Equivalently, use the anonymous constructor `âں¨val, isLtâں©`.
   165â†’
   166â†’## Example
   167â†’`âں¨3, by omegaâں© : Fin 5`
   168â†’-/
   169â†’DefinitionDoc Fin.mk as "Fin.mk"
   170â†’
   171â†’NewTactic omega exact rw intro cases آ«haveآ» use left right rfl constructor apply
   172â†’NewDefinition Fin Fin.mk
   173â†’
   174â†’DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto
   175â†’

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 1

Title "What is Fin n?"

Introduction "
# What is `Fin n`?

In mathematics, we often work with finite index sets like
$\\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\\}$ â€” the first $n$ natural numbers.

In Lean 4, this is captured by the type `Fin n`. An element of `Fin n`
is a natural number `k` **together with a proof** that `k < n`.

More precisely:
$$\\texttt{Fin } n = \\{ k : \\mathbb{N} \\mid k < n \\}$$

**Why not just use `â„•`?** If you index a length-5 vector with a plain
natural number, nothing stops you from using `7` â€” the type checker
can't tell it's out of bounds. With `Fin 5`, the bound is part of the
type: you literally *cannot construct* an element unless you prove it's
in range. The type system enforces the constraint for you.

You might wonder why `Fin n` starts at 0 rather than 1.
Zero-indexing means the values of `Fin n` are exactly the natural
numbers less than `n`, which aligns with how Lean indexes lists and
arrays. It also means `Fin n` has a natural 'zero' element when
`n > 0`.

This is an example of a *subtype* â€” a type carved out of a larger type
by a predicate. `Fin n` is the subtype `{ k : â„• // k < n }`.

To construct an element of `Fin n`, use the **anonymous constructor**
`âں¨k, proofâں©`, where:
- `k` is the natural number
- `proof` shows `k < n`

For the proof part, the `omega` tactic handles routine arithmetic
goals like `3 < 5`.

**Your task**: Construct any element of `Fin 5`. There are five to
choose from: $0, 1, 2, 3, 4$.

Try `exact âں¨3, by omegaâں©`.

*Notation*: type `âں¨` as `\\langle` and `âں©` as `\\rangle`.

**Important**: Lean happens to accept `exact 3` for this level
because it can figure out the bound automatically when it's a
concrete number like `5`. But this shortcut will **stop working**
starting at Level 12, when the bound becomes a variable `n`.
Practice `âں¨k, by omegaâں©` now â€” it's the pattern that always works.
"

-- INTENTIONAL DESIGN EXCEPTION (P0-1 accepted):
-- The goal type `Fin 5` allows `exact 0`, `exact 3`, etc. via Lean's
-- OfNat coercion. This is deliberately accepted as an onboarding choice:
--
-- Rationale: This is the very first level of the entire course. Forcing
-- the constructor pattern (e.g. via a variable bound or subtype wrapper)
-- would introduce additional complexity that overwhelms true beginners.
-- The introduction explicitly teaches `âں¨k, by omegaâں©` and asks the
-- learner to use it. The conclusion explicitly warns that the shortcut
-- (`exact 3`) only works for concrete bounds and that the explicit
-- constructor becomes mandatory from Level 12 onward.
--
-- The pedagogical contract: the level TEACHES the pattern without
-- FORCING it. The learner who follows instructions learns the right
-- skill; the learner who discovers the shortcut gets a preview of
-- Lean's coercion system, which is itself educational.
--
-- Alternatives considered and rejected:
-- - Variable bound (`Fin (n+1)`): `exact 0` still works via Zero instance
-- - Subtype wrapper (`{ i : Fin 5 // i.val = 3 }`): too complex for L01
-- - Disabling OfNat: not possible via DisabledTheorem

/-- Constructs a specific element of `Fin 5`. -/
Statement : Fin 5 := by
  Hint "Use the anonymous constructor `âں¨k, by omegaâں©` where `k` is any
  natural number less than 5. For example, `exact âں¨3, by omegaâں©`."
  exact âں¨3, by omegaâں©

Conclusion "
Well done! You just constructed the element $3$ as a term of type
`Fin 5`.

The key pattern: to build a `Fin n`, you provide **two things** â€” a
number and a proof that it's in range. The `âں¨k, by omegaâں©` idiom
will appear throughout this world.

In ordinary mathematics, saying *let $x = 3 \\in \\{0, \\ldots, 4\\}$*
is informal. In Lean, you must **prove** the element is in range.
That's what the second component does.

**Design note**: `exact 3` also works here â€” Lean can automatically
coerce a numeric literal into `Fin 5` when the bound is known.
We intentionally allow this shortcut in this first level so you can
focus on understanding the *structure* of `Fin` without getting stuck
on syntax. But practice the `âں¨k, by omegaâں©` pattern now, because
starting in Level 12, the type bound will be a variable `n` â€” not a
concrete number â€” and the automatic coercion won't work. The explicit
constructor is the general-purpose tool.
"

/-- `omega` closes goals involving linear arithmetic over `â„•` and `â„¤`.
It handles equalities (`=`), inequalities (`<`, `â‰¤`, `â‰¥`, `>`),
and negated equalities (`â‰ `).

## Syntax
```
omega
```

## When to use it
- When the goal follows from linear arithmetic: `3 < 5`, `n + 1 â‰¤ m`
- When hypotheses contradict each other: if you have `h1 : x = 5` and
  `h2 : x < 5`, omega sees the contradiction and closes any goal
- When multiple hypotheses combine: omega reasons across ALL hypotheses
  simultaneously, not just one at a time

## Examples
```
-- Goal: 3 < 5
omega

-- h1 : x = 5, h2 : x < 5 â†’ closes any goal (contradiction)
omega

-- h1 : a â‰¤ b, h2 : b < c â†’ Goal: a < c
omega
```

## Warning
`omega` only handles *linear* arithmetic. It cannot solve goals
involving multiplication of variables (like `n * m < n * n + 1`).
-/
TacticDoc omega

/-- `Fin n` is the type of natural numbers strictly less than `n`.

An element of `Fin n` consists of:
- `val : â„•` â€” the underlying natural number
- `isLt : val < n` â€” a proof that the number is in range

`Fin n` is the subtype `{ k : â„• // k < n }`.

## Construction
Use the anonymous constructor: `âں¨k, proofâں©`

## Example
`âں¨3, by omegaâں© : Fin 5` â€” the number 3 with a proof that `3 < 5`.

## Warning
`Fin n` has elements `0, 1, ..., n-1` (NOT `1, ..., n`).
`Fin 0` is **empty** â€” there is no natural number less than 0.
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin as "Fin"

/-- `Fin.mk val isLt` constructs an element of `Fin n` from a natural
number `val` and a proof `isLt : val < n`.

Equivalently, use the anonymous constructor `âں¨val, isLtâں©`.

## Example
`âں¨3, by omegaâں© : Fin 5`
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.mk as "Fin.mk"

NewTactic omega exact rw intro cases آ«haveآ» use left right rfl constructor apply
NewDefinition Fin Fin.mk

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 13

Title "The Empty Type"

Introduction "
# Fin 0 Has No Elements

What is `Fin 0`? It's the type of natural numbers less than 0.
But no natural number is less than 0, so **`Fin 0` is empty**.

This means: if you ever have `i : Fin 0` in your context, you have
a contradiction. From a contradiction, you can prove anything â€”
including `False`.

The key insight: `i.isLt` gives a proof of `i.val < 0`, but no
natural number satisfies this. So `omega` can derive `False` from
the contradictory hypothesis.

**Your task**: Given `i : Fin 0`, prove `False`.

Strategy: extract the impossible bound with `have h := i.isLt`,
then close with `omega`.
"

/-- An element of `Fin 0` leads to a contradiction. -/
Statement (i : Fin 0) : False := by
  Hint "Extract the bound proof: `have h := i.isLt`. This gives
  you `h : i.val < 0`, which is impossible for a natural number."
  have h := i.isLt
  Hint "Now `{h}` says a natural number is less than 0. `omega` can
  see this is impossible."
  omega

Conclusion "
`Fin 0` is the **empty type** â€” it has no elements at all.

If you ever have `i : Fin 0` in your hypotheses, you're in a
contradictory context. The `.isLt` field gives `i.val < 0`, and
since no natural number is less than 0, `omega` derives `False`.

This pattern â€” extract `.isLt`, close with `omega` â€” is the **Fin
contradiction pattern**. You already used it in Level 3 (where
`x.val = 5` contradicted `x.val < 5`). You'll use it again in the
case analysis levels ahead, where impossible branches have a value
too large for the bound.

This is an important edge case to keep in mind: `Fin n` for `n = 0`
is qualitatively different from `Fin n` for `n â‰¥ 1`.

*Alternative approach*: `exact Fin.elim0 i` also works â€” it's a
built-in function that eliminates any `Fin 0` element.
"

     9â†’# Destructuring Fin Elements
    10â†’
    11â†’So far, you've accessed the parts of a `Fin n` element using dot
    12â†’notation: `x.val` and `x.isLt`. There's another way:
    13â†’**destructuring** with `cases`.
    14â†’
    15â†’When you write:
    16â†’```
    17â†’cases x with | mk v hlt =>
    18â†’```
    19â†’Lean replaces `x : Fin 3` with two new hypotheses:
    20â†’- `v : â„•` â€” the underlying value
    21â†’- `hlt : v < 3` â€” the bound proof
    22â†’
    23â†’This is the same information as `x.val` and `x.isLt`, but now they're
    24â†’standalone variables in your context.
    25â†’

    27â†’you'll need to **case-split on the value itself** â€” asking whether
    28â†’`v = 0`, `v = 1`, or something else. That requires `v` as a standalone
    29â†’variable, which destructuring provides.
    30â†’

    32â†’from a natural number and a bound proof. In other words, show there
    33â†’exist `v` and `h : v < 3` such that `x = âں¨v, hâں©`.
    34â†’
    35â†’Destructure `x` first â€” after that, the components are right there in
    36â†’your context and you can package them up.
    37â†’"
    38â†’
    39â†’/-- Every Fin element can be reconstructed from its components. -/
    40â†’Statement (x : Fin 3) : âˆƒ (v : â„•) (h : v < 3), x = âں¨v, hâں© := by
    41â†’  Hint "Destructure `x` into its components:
    42â†’  `cases x with | mk v hlt =>`"
    43â†’  cases x with
    44â†’  | mk v hlt =>
    45â†’    Hint "Now you have `v : â„•` and `hlt : v < 3` as standalone
    46â†’    variables. The goal asks for a number and a bound proof such that
    47â†’    the element equals the reconstruction. You already have both!
    48â†’    Use `exact âں¨v, hlt, rflâں©` to provide `v` as the number, `hlt`
    49â†’    as the bound proof, and `rfl` for the equality (since the element
    50â†’    IS its own reconstruction)."
    51â†’    exact âں¨v, hlt, rflâں©
    52â†’
    53â†’Conclusion "
    54â†’You've just destructured a `Fin` element for the first time.
    55â†’
    56â†’The pattern `cases x with | mk v hlt =>` is the `Fin` version of
    57â†’a general Lean pattern: any structure (or subtype) can be taken
    58â†’apart with `cases` to expose its components.
    59â†’
    60â†’| Before destructuring | After |
    61â†’|---|---|
    62â†’| `x : Fin 3` | `v : â„•`, `hlt : v < 3` |
    63â†’| `x.val` | `v` |
    64â†’| `x.isLt` | `hlt` |
    65â†’
    66â†’After destructuring, the components are standalone variables that
    67â†’you can use directly â€” as existential witnesses, in case splits, or
    68â†’in any other tactic that needs a named term. The equality
    69â†’`âں¨v, hltâں© = âں¨v, hltâں©` closes by `rfl` because the element IS its
    70â†’own reconstruction from components.
    71â†’
    72â†’**Alternative: `obtain`**. There is another way to destructure in
    73â†’Lean: `obtain âں¨v, hltâں© := x`. This does the same thing as
    74â†’`cases x with | mk v hlt =>` when the type has exactly one
    75â†’constructor (as `Fin` does â€” its only constructor is `mk`). Use

    80â†’
    81â†’You'll need destructuring in the next level for full case analysis.

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 16

Title "Destructuring Fin"

Introduction "
# Destructuring Fin Elements

So far, you've accessed the parts of a `Fin n` element using dot
notation: `x.val` and `x.isLt`. There's another way:
**destructuring** with `cases`.

When you write:
```
cases x with | mk v hlt =>
```
Lean replaces `x : Fin 3` with two new hypotheses:
- `v : â„•` â€” the underlying value
- `hlt : v < 3` â€” the bound proof

This is the same information as `x.val` and `x.isLt`, but now they're
standalone variables in your context.

Why learn a second way to access these? Because in the next level,
you'll need to **case-split on the value itself** â€” asking whether
`v = 0`, `v = 1`, or something else. That requires `v` as a standalone
variable, which destructuring provides.

**Your task**: Prove that every element of `Fin 3` can be reconstructed
from a natural number and a bound proof. In other words, show there
exist `v` and `h : v < 3` such that `x = âں¨v, hâں©`.

Destructure `x` first â€” after that, the components are right there in
your context and you can package them up.
"

/-- Every Fin element can be reconstructed from its components. -/
Statement (x : Fin 3) : âˆƒ (v : â„•) (h : v < 3), x = âں¨v, hâں© := by
  Hint "Destructure `x` into its components:
  `cases x with | mk v hlt =>`"
  cases x with
  | mk v hlt =>
    Hint "Now you have `v : â„•` and `hlt : v < 3` as standalone
    variables. The goal asks for a number and a bound proof such that
    the element equals the reconstruction. You already have both!
    Use `exact âں¨v, hlt, rflâں©` to provide `v` as the number, `hlt`
    as the bound proof, and `rfl` for the equality (since the element
    IS its own reconstruction)."
    exact âں¨v, hlt, rflâں©

Conclusion "
You've just destructured a `Fin` element for the first time.

The pattern `cases x with | mk v hlt =>` is the `Fin` version of
a general Lean pattern: any structure (or subtype) can be taken
apart with `cases` to expose its components.

| Before destructuring | After |
|---|---|
| `x : Fin 3` | `v : â„•`, `hlt : v < 3` |
| `x.val` | `v` |
| `x.isLt` | `hlt` |

After destructuring, the components are standalone variables that
you can use directly â€” as existential witnesses, in case splits, or
in any other tactic that needs a named term. The equality
`âں¨v, hltâں© = âں¨v, hltâں©` closes by `rfl` because the element IS its
own reconstruction from components.

**Alternative: `obtain`**. There is another way to destructure in
Lean: `obtain âں¨v, hltâں© := x`. This does the same thing as
`cases x with | mk v hlt =>` when the type has exactly one
constructor (as `Fin` does â€” its only constructor is `mk`). Use
`cases` when you need to enumerate multiple constructors (like
`Nat.zero` and `Nat.succ`); use `obtain` when there's only one
constructor and you just want to extract the components. Both
are common in real Lean code.

You'll need destructuring in the next level for full case analysis.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 22

Title "Existential Witnesses"

Introduction "
# Providing a Fin Element as a Witness

In Level 1, you constructed a `Fin` element with `exact âں¨k, by omegaâں©`.
But what if the goal asks you to prove that *some* element exists
with a property? Then you need `use` to provide the witness.

When the goal is `âˆƒ x : Fin n, P x`, the tactic `use âں¨k, by omegaâں©`
provides a specific `Fin n` element as the witness, then asks you
to prove `P` holds for that element.

This combines two skills you already know:
- `use` from the NNG4 prerequisites (providing existential witnesses)
- `âں¨k, by omegaâں©` from Level 1 (constructing `Fin` elements)

**Your task**: Find a `Fin 5` element whose value is `3`.
"

/-- There exists an element of `Fin 5` with value `3`. -/
Statement : âˆƒ x : Fin 5, x.val = 3 := by
  Hint "Provide the witness with `use âں¨3, by omegaâں©`. This constructs
  the element `3 : Fin 5` and provides it as the existential witness.
  The remaining goal `3 = 3` closes automatically."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`use âں¨3, by omegaâں©` provides the witness and
  Lean closes `3 = 3` by reflexivity."
  use âں¨3, by omegaâں©

Conclusion "
The pattern `use âں¨k, by omegaâں©` provides a `Fin` element as an
existential witness. This is simply the combination of two moves:

| Move | From |
|---|---|
| `use expr` â€” provide a witness | NNG4 prerequisites |
| `âں¨k, by omegaâں©` â€” construct a Fin element | Level 1 |

You'll use this combination in the boss.
"

1â†’import Game.Metadata
     2â†’
     3â†’World "FinNavigation"
     4â†’Level 1
     5â†’
     6â†’Title "The Last Element"
     7â†’
     8â†’Introduction "
     9â†’# Fin.last: The Boundary Element
    10â†’
    11â†’In the previous world, you learned that `Fin n` contains the numbers
    12â†’$0, 1, \\ldots, n-1$. But how do you name the *largest* element?
    13â†’
    14â†’For `Fin (n+1)`, the largest element has value `n`. Lean gives it a
    15â†’name: **`Fin.last n`**.
    16â†’
    17â†’`Fin.last n` lives in `Fin (n+1)` â€” not `Fin n` â€” because `n` is
    18â†’less than `n+1` but not less than `n`.
    19â†’
    20â†’The theorem **`Fin.val_last`** extracts its value:
    21â†’$$\\texttt{(Fin.last } n\\texttt{).val} = n$$
    22â†’
    23â†’**Your task**: Show that `Fin.last 4` has value `4`.
    24â†’"
    25â†’
    26â†’/-- `Fin.last 4` has value 4. -/
    27â†’Statement : (Fin.last 4).val = 4 := by
    28â†’  Hint "Use `exact Fin.val_last 4` to close this goal.
    29â†’  (Since the definition is transparent, `rfl` also works here.
    30â†’  But `Fin.val_last` is the general tool you'll need when `n`
    31â†’  is a variable.)"
    32â†’  exact Fin.val_last 4
    33â†’
    34â†’Conclusion "
    35â†’`Fin.val_last n` tells you that `(Fin.last n).val = n`.
    36â†’
    37â†’This is the analog of saying: the largest element of
    38â†’$\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n\\}$ is $n$. In Lean, you need `Fin.val_last`
    39â†’to access this fact because `Fin.last` is an opaque term â€”
    40â†’the type system doesn't automatically compute its value.
    41â†’
    42â†’In the next level, you'll use `Fin.val_last` as a **rewrite** tool
    43â†’inside a larger proof.
    44â†’"
    45â†’
    46â†’/-- `Fin.last n` is the largest element of `Fin (n + 1)`.
    47â†’Its underlying value is `n`.
    48â†’
    49â†’## Construction
    50â†’`Fin.last n : Fin (n + 1)`
    51â†’
    52â†’## Example
    53â†’`Fin.last 4 : Fin 5` â€” the element with value 4.
    54â†’
    55â†’## Value
    56â†’`(Fin.last n).val = n` (see `Fin.val_last`).
    57â†’-/
    58â†’DefinitionDoc Fin.last as "Fin.last"
    59â†’
    60â†’/-- `Fin.val_last n` states that `(Fin.last n).val = n`.
    61â†’
    62â†’## Syntax
    63â†’```
    64â†’rw [Fin.val_last]    -- rewrites (Fin.last n).val to n

import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 1

Title "The Last Element"

Introduction "
# Fin.last: The Boundary Element

In the previous world, you learned that `Fin n` contains the numbers
$0, 1, \\ldots, n-1$. But how do you name the *largest* element?

For `Fin (n+1)`, the largest element has value `n`. Lean gives it a
name: **`Fin.last n`**.

`Fin.last n` lives in `Fin (n+1)` â€” not `Fin n` â€” because `n` is
less than `n+1` but not less than `n`.

The theorem **`Fin.val_last`** extracts its value:
$$\\texttt{(Fin.last } n\\texttt{).val} = n$$

**Your task**: Show that `Fin.last 4` has value `4`.
"

/-- `Fin.last 4` has value 4. -/
Statement : (Fin.last 4).val = 4 := by
  Hint "Use `exact Fin.val_last 4` to close this goal.
  (Since the definition is transparent, `rfl` also works here.
  But `Fin.val_last` is the general tool you'll need when `n`
  is a variable.)"
  exact Fin.val_last 4

Conclusion "
`Fin.val_last n` tells you that `(Fin.last n).val = n`.

This is the analog of saying: the largest element of
$\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n\\}$ is $n$. In Lean, you need `Fin.val_last`
to access this fact because `Fin.last` is an opaque term â€”
the type system doesn't automatically compute its value.

In the next level, you'll use `Fin.val_last` as a **rewrite** tool
inside a larger proof.
"

/-- `Fin.last n` is the largest element of `Fin (n + 1)`.
Its underlying value is `n`.

## Construction
`Fin.last n : Fin (n + 1)`

## Example
`Fin.last 4 : Fin 5` â€” the element with value 4.

## Value
`(Fin.last n).val = n` (see `Fin.val_last`).
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.last as "Fin.last"

/-- `Fin.val_last n` states that `(Fin.last n).val = n`.

## Syntax
```
rw [Fin.val_last]    -- rewrites (Fin.last n).val to n
exact Fin.val_last n -- closes a goal (Fin.last n).val = n
```

## When to use it
When you need to compute or simplify the value of `Fin.last`.

## Example
```
-- Goal: (Fin.last 4).val = 4
exact Fin.val_last 4
```
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.val_last as "Fin.val_last" in "Fin"

NewDefinition Fin.last
NewTheorem Fin.val_last

import Game.Metadata

World "FinTuples"
Level 1

Title "Functions as Tuples"

Introduction "
# Functions from Fin as Tuples

In the previous worlds, you learned that `Fin n` is the type of
natural numbers less than `n`. Now comes a key insight:

**A function `Fin n â†’ خ±` is the same thing as an `n`-tuple** â€” an
ordered list of `n` elements from `خ±`.

Why? A function from `Fin 3 â†’ â„•` is determined by three values:
what it returns at `0`, at `1`, and at `2`. Those three values, in
order, form a triple. Conversely, any triple $(a_0, a_1, a_2)$
defines a function `Fin 3 â†’ â„•` by $i \\mapsto a_i$.

In Lean, `![10, 20, 30]` creates a function `Fin 3 â†’ â„•` that
returns `10` at index `0`, `20` at index `1`, and `30` at index `2`.
The `!` prefix distinguishes this from a plain list â€” it's a
**vector** (a function from `Fin n`), not a linked list.

**Your task**: Verify that the element at index 1 of `![10, 20, 30]`
is `20`. (Remember: indexing starts at 0.)
"

/-- The element at index 1 of `![10, 20, 30]` is 20. -/
Statement : (![10, 20, 30] : Fin 3 â†’ â„•) 1 = 20 := by
  Hint "The value `![10, 20, 30] 1` reduces to `20` by definition.
  Use `rfl`."
  rfl

Conclusion "
You just accessed an element of a 3-tuple. The key idea:

$$\\texttt{![10, 20, 30]}$$

creates a function `Fin 3 â†’ â„•`. Evaluating it at index `1` gives
`20` â€” the second element (counting from 0).

This is analogous to subscript notation in mathematics:
if $v = (10, 20, 30)$, then $v_1 = 20$.
In Lean, `v 1` plays the role of $v_1$.

Since the value is determined by the definition, `rfl` closes
the equation.

Notice the type: `Fin 3 â†’ â„•`. The domain `Fin 3` has exactly 3
elements, so the function is completely determined by 3 values â€”
it's a 3-tuple. More generally, `Fin n â†’ خ±` is an `n`-tuple. The
number of elements in the tuple IS the number of elements in `Fin n`.
This connection between tuple length and `Fin n` cardinality will be
formalized in the Cardinality world.
"

1â†’import Game.Metadata
     2â†’
     3â†’World "FinsetBasics"
     4â†’Level 1
     5â†’
     6â†’Title "What is a Finset?"
     7â†’
     8â†’Introduction "
     9â†’# What is a Finset?
    10â†’
    11â†’A `Finset خ±` is a finite set of elements from type `خ±`. Unlike tuples
    12â†’(`Fin n â†’ خ±`), finsets are **unordered** and have **no duplicates**.
    13â†’
    14â†’You can write concrete finsets using literal notation:
    15â†’- `{1, 2, 3} : Finset â„•` â€” a finset of three natural numbers
    16â†’- `{true, false} : Finset Bool` â€” a finset of booleans
    17â†’
    18â†’**Why DecidableEq?** Finsets require `DecidableEq خ±` â€” Lean must be
    19â†’able to decide whether two elements are equal. Why? Because maintaining
    20â†’'no duplicates' requires checking whether an inserted element is already
    21â†’present. If you can't decide `a = b`, you can't check for duplicates.
    22â†’This is why `Finset â„‌` doesn't exist in general â€” real number equality
    23â†’isn't decidable.
    24â†’
    25â†’Under the hood, `{1, 2, 3}` is sugar for nested `insert` calls:
    26â†’$$\\{1, 2, 3\\} = \\texttt{insert } 1 \\;(\\texttt{insert } 2 \\;\\{3\\})$$
    27â†’
    28â†’where `{3}` is a *singleton* â€” a set with exactly one element.
    29â†’
    30â†’The fundamental question for any set: **is this element a member?**
    31â†’
    32â†’The key lemma is `Finset.mem_insert`:
    33â†’$$a \\in \\texttt{insert } b \\; s \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; a = b \\;\\lor\\; a \\in s$$
    34â†’
    35â†’In words: `a` is in `insert b s` if either `a = b` (it's the inserted
    36â†’element) or `a` was already in `s`.
    37â†’
    38â†’**Your task**: Prove that $1 \\in \\{1, 2, 3\\}$.
    39â†’
    40â†’Use `rw [Finset.mem_insert]` to unfold the membership test. Since
    41â†’`{1, 2, 3}` is `insert 1 (insert 2 {3})`, this rewrites the goal to
    42â†’`1 = 1 âˆ¨ 1 âˆˆ {2, 3}`. Then choose the left branch with `left` and
    43â†’close with `rfl`.
    44â†’"
    45â†’
    46â†’/-- 1 is an element of the finset {1, 2, 3}. -/
    47â†’Statement : (1 : â„•) âˆˆ ({1, 2, 3} : Finset â„•) := by
    48â†’  Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_insert]` to convert the membership goal into
    49â†’  a disjunction: is `1` the inserted element, or is it in the rest?"
    50â†’  rw [Finset.mem_insert]
    51â†’  Hint "The goal is now a disjunction: `1 = 1` on the left, or
    52â†’  `1` is in the remaining set on the right. Since `1 = 1` is true,
    53â†’  choose the left branch with `left`."
    54â†’  left
    55â†’  Hint "The goal is `1 = 1`. Close it with `rfl`."
    56â†’  rfl
    57â†’
    58â†’Conclusion "
    59â†’You just proved set membership by **peeling off** the outermost
    60â†’`insert` using `Finset.mem_insert`. The pattern:
    61â†’
    62â†’1. `rw [Finset.mem_insert]` â€” converts `a âˆˆ insert b s` to `a = b âˆ¨ a âˆˆ s`
    63â†’2. `left` or `right` â€” choose which branch
    64â†’3. `rfl` or continue peeling
    65â†’
    66â†’This is the fundamental membership proof move for finsets.
    67â†’
    68â†’Since `1` was the *first* element inserted, one peel was enough.
    69â†’What if the element you're looking for is deeper in the set?
    70â†’That's the next level.
    71â†’"
    72â†’
    73â†’/-- `Finset خ±` is a finite set of elements from type `خ±`.
    74â†’
    75â†’A `Finset` is an unordered collection with no duplicates and
    76â†’decidable membership.
    77â†’
    78â†’## Construction
    79â†’- `{a, b, c} : Finset خ±` â€” literal notation (requires `DecidableEq خ±`)
    80â†’- `âˆ… : Finset خ±` â€” the empty finset (contains no elements)
    81â†’- `{a} : Finset خ±` â€” a *singleton* finset (exactly one element)
    82â†’- `insert a s` â€” add element `a` to finset `s` (no-op if `a âˆˆ s`)
    83â†’- `Finset.range n` â€” the set `{0, 1, ..., n-1}`
    84â†’
    85â†’## How literal notation works
    86â†’`{1, 2, 3}` is sugar for `insert 1 (insert 2 {3})`:
    87â†’- The outermost layer is an `insert`
    88â†’- Each nested layer is another `insert`
    89â†’- The innermost `{3}` is a singleton
    90â†’
    91â†’## Membership
    92â†’`x âˆˆ s` is decidable for any `Finset`.
    93â†’
    94â†’## Key difference from tuples
    95â†’Tuples (`Fin n â†’ خ±`) are ordered and accessed by index.
    96â†’Finsets are unordered and accessed by membership.
    97â†’-/
    98â†’DefinitionDoc Finset as "Finset"
    99â†’
   100â†’/-- `Finset.mem_insert` states that `a âˆˆ insert b s â†” a = b âˆ¨ a âˆˆ s`.
   101â†’
   102â†’## Syntax
   103â†’```
   104â†’rw [Finset.mem_insert]       -- unfold membership in the goal
   105â†’rw [Finset.mem_insert] at h  -- unfold membership in hypothesis h
   106â†’```
   107â†’
   108â†’## When to use it
   109â†’When the goal or a hypothesis involves `x âˆˆ insert a s` (which includes
   110â†’literal finsets like `x âˆˆ {1, 2, 3}`, since `{1, 2, 3} = insert 1 (insert 2 {3})`).
   111â†’
   112â†’## Example
   113â†’```
   114â†’-- Goal: 1 âˆˆ {1, 2, 3}
   115â†’rw [Finset.mem_insert]
   116â†’-- Goal: 1 = 1 âˆ¨ 1 âˆˆ {2, 3}
   117â†’left
   118â†’rfl
   119â†’```
   120â†’-/
   121â†’TheoremDoc Finset.mem_insert as "Finset.mem_insert" in "Finset"
   122â†’
   123â†’/-- `Finset.insert a s` adds element `a` to finset `s`.
   124â†’
   125â†’If `a` is already in `s`, the result is unchanged (no duplicates).
   126â†’
   127â†’## Notation
   128â†’`{1, 2, 3}` is sugar for `insert 1 (insert 2 {3})`:
   129â†’- Each comma corresponds to one `insert`
   130â†’- The innermost element is a singleton
   131â†’
   132â†’## Key lemma
   133â†’`Finset.mem_insert : a âˆˆ insert b s â†” a = b âˆ¨ a âˆˆ s`
   134â†’
   135â†’## Idempotency
   136â†’If `a âˆˆ s`, then `insert a s = s` (see `Finset.insert_eq_of_mem`).
   137â†’-/
   138â†’DefinitionDoc Finset.insert as "Finset.insert"
   139â†’
   140â†’TheoremTab "Finset"
   141â†’/-- `Insert.insert a s` is the type class method underlying the `insert` notation.
   142â†’For finsets, `insert a s` adds element `a` to finset `s`. -/
   143â†’DefinitionDoc Insert.insert as "Insert.insert"
   144â†’
   145â†’NewDefinition Finset Finset.insert Insert.insert

file.content:
import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetBasics"
Level 1

Title "What is a Finset?"

Introduction "
# What is a Finset?

A `Finset خ±` is a finite set of elements from type `خ±`. Unlike tuples
(`Fin n â†’ خ±`), finsets are **unordered** and have **no duplicates**.

You can write concrete finsets using literal notation:
- `{1, 2, 3} : Finset â„•` â€” a finset of three natural numbers
- `{true, false} : Finset Bool` â€” a finset of booleans

**Why DecidableEq?** Finsets require `DecidableEq خ±` â€” Lean must be
able to decide whether two elements are equal. Why? Because maintaining
'no duplicates' requires checking whether an inserted element is already
present. If you can't decide `a = b`, you can't check for duplicates.
This is why `Finset â„‌` doesn't exist in general â€” real number equality
isn't decidable.

Under the hood, `{1, 2, 3}` is sugar for nested `insert` calls:
$$\\{1, 2, 3\\} = \\texttt{insert } 1 \\;(\\texttt{insert } 2 \\;\\{3\\})$$

where `{3}` is a *singleton* â€” a set with exactly one element.

The fundamental question for any set: **is this element a member?**

The key lemma is `Finset.mem_insert`:
$$a \\in \\texttt{insert } b \\; s \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; a = b \\;\\lor\\; a \\in s$$

In words: `a` is in `insert b s` if either `a = b` (it's the inserted
element) or `a` was already in `s`.

**Your task**: Prove that $1 \\in \\{1, 2, 3\\}$.

Use `rw [Finset.mem_insert]` to unfold the membership test. Since
`{1, 2, 3}` is `insert 1 (insert 2 {3})`, this rewrites the goal to
`1 = 1 âˆ¨ 1 âˆˆ {2, 3}`. Then choose the left branch with `left` and
close with `rfl`.
"

/-- 1 is an element of the finset {1, 2, 3}. -/
Statement : (1 : â„•) âˆˆ ({1, 2, 3} : Finset â„•) := by
  Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_insert]` to convert the membership goal into
  a disjunction: is `1` the inserted element, or is it in the rest?"
  rw [Finset.mem_insert]
  Hint "The goal is now a disjunction: `1 = 1` on the left, or
  `1` is in the remaining set on the right. Since `1 = 1` is true,
  choose the left branch with `left`."
  left
  Hint "The goal is `1 = 1`. Close it with `rfl`."
  rfl

Conclusion "
You just proved set membership by **peeling off** the outermost
`insert` using `Finset.mem_insert`. The pattern:

1. `rw [Finset.mem_insert]` â€” converts `a âˆˆ insert b s` to `a = b âˆ¨ a âˆˆ s`
2. `left` or `right` â€” choose which branch
3. `rfl` or continue peeling

This is the fundamental membership proof move for finsets.

Since `1` was the *first* element inserted, one peel was enough.
What if the element you're looking for is deeper in the set?
That's the next level.
"

/-- `Finset خ±` is a finite set of elements from type `خ±`.

A `Finset` is an unordered collection with no duplicates and
decidable membership.

## Construction
- `{a, b, c} : Finset خ±` â€” literal notation (requires `DecidableEq خ±`)
- `âˆ… : Finset خ±` â€” the empty finset (contains no elements)
- `{a} : Finset خ±` â€” a *singleton* finset (exactly one element)
- `insert a s` â€” add element `a` to finset `s` (no-op if `a âˆˆ s`)
- `Finset.range n` â€” the set `{0, 1, ..., n-1}`

## How literal notation works
`{1, 2, 3}` is sugar for `insert 1 (insert 2 {3})`:
- The outermost layer is an `insert`
- Each nested layer is another `insert`
- The innermost `{3}` is a singleton

## Membership
`x âˆˆ s` is decidable for any `Finset`.

## Key difference from tuples
Tuples (`Fin n â†’ خ±`) are ordered and accessed by index.
Finsets are unordered and accessed by membership.
-/
DefinitionDoc Finset as "Finset"

/-- `Finset.mem_insert` states that `a âˆˆ insert b s â†” a = b âˆ¨ a âˆˆ s`.

## Syntax
```
rw [Finset.mem_insert]       -- unfold membership in the goal
rw [Finset.mem_insert] at h  -- unfold membership in hypothesis h
```

## When to use it
When the goal or a hypothesis involves `x âˆˆ insert a s` (which includes
literal finsets like `x âˆˆ {1, 2, 3}`, since `{1, 2, 3} = insert 1 (insert 2 {3})`).

## Example
```
-- Goal: 1 âˆˆ {1, 2, 3}
rw [Finset.mem_insert]
-- Goal: 1 = 1 âˆ¨ 1 âˆˆ {2, 3}
left
rfl
```
-/
TheoremDoc Finset.mem_insert as "Finset.mem_insert" in "Finset"

/-- `Finset.insert a s` adds element `a` to finset `s`.

If `a` is already in `s`, the result is unchanged (no duplicates).

## Notation
`{1, 2, 3}` is sugar for `insert 1 (insert 2 {3})`:
- Each comma corresponds to one `insert`
- The innermost element is a singleton

## Key lemma
`Finset.mem_insert : a âˆˆ insert b s â†” a = b âˆ¨ a âˆˆ s`

## Idempotency
If `a âˆˆ s`, then `insert a s = s` (see `Finset.insert_eq_of_mem`).
-/
DefinitionDoc Finset.insert as "Finset.insert"

TheoremTab "Finset"
/-- `Insert.insert a s` is the type class method underlying the `insert` notation.
For finsets, `insert a s` adds element `a` to finset `s`. -/
DefinitionDoc Insert.insert as "Insert.insert"

NewDefinition Finset Finset.insert Insert.insert
NewTheorem Finset.mem_insert

1â†’import Game.Metadata
     2â†’
     3â†’World "FinsetOperations"
     4â†’Level 1
     5â†’
     6â†’Title "Union Membership"
     7â†’
     8â†’Introduction "
     9â†’# Union: Combining Two Finsets
    10â†’
    11â†’The **union** `s âˆھ t` contains every element that belongs to `s`, to `t`,
    12â†’or to both. The membership characterization is:
    13â†’
    14â†’$$a \\in s \\cup t \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; a \\in s \\;\\lor\\; a \\in t$$
    15â†’
    16â†’In Lean, this is `Finset.mem_union`:
    17â†’```
    18â†’Finset.mem_union : a âˆˆ s âˆھ t â†” a âˆˆ s âˆ¨ a âˆˆ t
    19â†’```
    20â†’
    21â†’The proof move: `rw [Finset.mem_union]` converts a union membership goal
    22â†’into a disjunction. Then you choose `left` or `right` and prove membership
    23â†’in the appropriate piece.
    24â†’
    25â†’**Note**: The `ext` tactic is temporarily unavailable in Levels 1-7.
    26â†’You know `ext` from earlier worlds, and it IS the standard tool for
    27â†’finset equality proofs. But these first levels focus on the membership
    28â†’characterization lemmas â€” the building blocks that `ext` proofs are
    29â†’built from. You need to master `rw [Finset.mem_union]`, `rw [Finset.mem_inter]`,
    30â†’etc. before combining them via `ext`. The tactic returns in Level 8.
    31â†’
    32â†’**Your task**: Prove that $3 \\in \\{1, 2\\} \\cup \\{3, 4\\}$.
    33â†’

import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetOperations"
Level 1

Title "Union Membership"

Introduction "
# Union: Combining Two Finsets

The **union** `s âˆھ t` contains every element that belongs to `s`, to `t`,
or to both. The membership characterization is:

$$a \\in s \\cup t \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; a \\in s \\;\\lor\\; a \\in t$$

In Lean, this is `Finset.mem_union`:
```
Finset.mem_union : a âˆˆ s âˆھ t â†” a âˆˆ s âˆ¨ a âˆˆ t
```

The proof move: `rw [Finset.mem_union]` converts a union membership goal
into a disjunction. Then you choose `left` or `right` and prove membership
in the appropriate piece.

**Note**: The `ext` tactic is temporarily unavailable in Levels 1-7.
You know `ext` from earlier worlds, and it IS the standard tool for
finset equality proofs. But these first levels focus on the membership
characterization lemmas â€” the building blocks that `ext` proofs are
built from. You need to master `rw [Finset.mem_union]`, `rw [Finset.mem_inter]`,
etc. before combining them via `ext`. The tactic returns in Level 8.

**Your task**: Prove that $3 \\in \\{1, 2\\} \\cup \\{3, 4\\}$.

Since $3$ is in the right-hand set, you'll choose `right` after
rewriting with `mem_union`, then prove $3 \\in \\{3, 4\\}$ using the
insert-peel pattern from Finset Basics.
"

/-- 3 is an element of {1, 2} âˆھ {3, 4}. -/
Statement : (3 : â„•) âˆˆ ({1, 2} : Finset â„•) âˆھ {3, 4} := by
  Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_union]` to convert the union membership
  into a disjunction: is `3` in the left set or the right set?"
  rw [Finset.mem_union]
  Hint "The goal is a disjunction: `3` is in the left set or the right
  set. Since $3$ is in the right set, choose `right`."
  right
  Hint "Now prove membership in the right set. Peel the outer insert with
  `rw [Finset.mem_insert]`."
  rw [Finset.mem_insert]
  Hint (hidden := true) "The goal is `3 = 3 âˆ¨ 3 âˆˆ singleton 4`.
  Choose `left` and close with `rfl`."
  left
  rfl

Conclusion "
The pattern for union membership:
1. `rw [Finset.mem_union]` â€” converts `a âˆˆ s âˆھ t` to `a âˆˆ s âˆ¨ a âˆˆ t`
2. `left` or `right` â€” choose which set the element is in
3. Prove membership in that set (using `mem_insert`/`mem_singleton` from Finset Basics)

This is the same rewrite-then-choose pattern you used for `mem_insert`,
but now at the level of entire sets rather than individual elements.

In plain mathematics: to show $x \\in A \\cup B$, either show $x \\in A$ or
$x \\in B$.
"

/-- `Finset.mem_union` states that `a âˆˆ s âˆھ t â†” a âˆˆ s âˆ¨ a âˆˆ t`.

## Syntax
```
rw [Finset.mem_union]       -- in the goal
rw [Finset.mem_union] at h  -- in a hypothesis
```

## When to use it
When the goal or a hypothesis involves `x âˆˆ s âˆھ t`. After rewriting,
the goal becomes a disjunction that you handle with `left`/`right`.

## Example
```
-- Goal: 3 âˆˆ {1, 2} âˆھ {3, 4}
rw [Finset.mem_union]
-- Goal: 3 âˆˆ {1, 2} âˆ¨ 3 âˆˆ {3, 4}
right
-- Goal: 3 âˆˆ {3, 4}
```
-/
TheoremDoc Finset.mem_union as "Finset.mem_union" in "Finset"

/-- `Finset.union s t` (written `s âˆھ t`) is the finset containing all
elements that belong to `s`, to `t`, or to both.

## Notation
`s âˆھ t` â€” the union of finsets `s` and `t`.

## Key lemma
`Finset.mem_union : a âˆˆ s âˆھ t â†” a âˆˆ s âˆ¨ a âˆˆ t`

## Properties
- Union is commutative: `s âˆھ t = t âˆھ s`
- Union is associative: `(s âˆھ t) âˆھ u = s âˆھ (t âˆھ u)`
- Union with empty: `s âˆھ âˆ… = s`
- Union is idempotent: `s âˆھ s = s`
-/
DefinitionDoc Finset.union as "Finset.union"

TheoremTab "Finset"
NewDefinition Finset.union
NewTheorem Finset.mem_union

DisabledTactic trivial آ«decideآ» native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto ext
DisabledTheorem Finset.mem_insert_self Finset.mem_insert_of_mem Finset.mem_union_left Finset.mem_union_right

    10â†’
    11â†’Given a function `f : خ± â†’ خ²` and a finset `s : Finset خ±`, the **image**
    12â†’`s.image f` (or `Finset.image f s`) is the finset of all values `f x`
    13â†’for `x âˆˆ s`. This is the formal version of the set-theoretic image
    14â†’$f(S) = \\{f(x) \\mid x \\in S\\}$.
    15â†’
    16â†’The key membership characterization is `Finset.mem_image`:
    17â†’```
    18â†’Finset.mem_image : y âˆˆ s.image f â†” âˆƒ x âˆˆ s, f x = y
    19â†’```
    20â†’

    23â†’2. `use x` â€” provide the witness (the preimage of `y`)

    27â†’

    29â†’And $2 \\in \\text{range}(4)$ since $2 < 4$.

    46â†’  rw [Finset.mem_range]
    47â†’  omega

    50â†’The image membership pattern:
    51â†’1. `rw [Finset.mem_image]` â€” reduces to an existential
    52â†’2. `use witness` â€” provide the preimage (Lean auto-verifies `f x = y`)
    53â†’3. Prove `x âˆˆ s` (the remaining goal)
    54â†’
    55â†’The challenge is step 2: choosing the right witness. You need to solve

    57â†’
    58â†’In plain math: to show $y \\in f(S)$, exhibit $x \\in S$ with $f(x) = y$.
    59â†’"
    60â†’
    61â†’/-- `Finset.image f s` is the finset of all values `f x` for `x âˆˆ s`.

import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetImage"
Level 1

Title "First Contact with Image"

Introduction "
# Finset.image: Applying a Function to Every Element

Given a function `f : خ± â†’ خ²` and a finset `s : Finset خ±`, the **image**
`s.image f` (or `Finset.image f s`) is the finset of all values `f x`
for `x âˆˆ s`. This is the formal version of the set-theoretic image
$f(S) = \\{f(x) \\mid x \\in S\\}$.

The key membership characterization is `Finset.mem_image`:
```
Finset.mem_image : y âˆˆ s.image f â†” âˆƒ x âˆˆ s, f x = y
```

To prove `y âˆˆ s.image f`, the proof recipe is:
1. `rw [Finset.mem_image]` â€” converts membership to an existential
2. `use x` â€” provide the witness (the preimage of `y`)
3. Prove that `x âˆˆ s` and `f x = y`

**Your task**: Prove that $3 \\in \\text{image}(n \\mapsto n+1,\\; \\{0,1,2,3\\})$.

The preimage of $3$ under $n \\mapsto n+1$ is $2$, since $2+1=3$.
And $2 \\in \\text{range}(4)$ since $2 < 4$.
"

/-- 3 is in the image of (n â†¦ n + 1) applied to range 4. -/
Statement : (3 : â„•) âˆˆ (Finset.range 4).image (fun n => n + 1) := by
  Hint "Use `rw [Finset.mem_image]` to convert the membership goal
  into an existential: you need to find some `x âˆˆ range 4` with
  `x + 1 = 3`."
  rw [Finset.mem_image]
  Hint "The goal is `âˆƒ x âˆˆ Finset.range 4, x + 1 = 3`.
  Which `x` satisfies `x + 1 = 3`? Solve: `x = 2`.
  Use `use 2` to provide the witness."
  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `use 2`."
  use 2
  Hint "After `use 2`, Lean automatically verified that `2 + 1 = 3`
  (by computation). The remaining goal is `2 âˆˆ Finset.range 4`.
  Rewrite with `rw [Finset.mem_range]` to get `2 < 4`, then `omega`."
  rw [Finset.mem_range]
  omega

Conclusion "
The image membership pattern:
1. `rw [Finset.mem_image]` â€” reduces to an existential
2. `use witness` â€” provide the preimage (Lean auto-verifies `f x = y`)
3. Prove `x âˆˆ s` (the remaining goal)

The challenge is step 2: choosing the right witness. You need to solve
$f(x) = y$ for $x$ â€” here, $x + 1 = 3$ gives $x = 2$.

In plain math: to show $y \\in f(S)$, exhibit $x \\in S$ with $f(x) = y$.
"

/-- `Finset.image f s` is the finset of all values `f x` for `x âˆˆ s`.

## Notation
`s.image f` or `Finset.image f s`

## Key lemma
`Finset.mem_image : y âˆˆ s.image f â†” âˆƒ x âˆˆ s, f x = y`

## Example
`(Finset.range 3).image (fun n => n * 2)` gives the finset containing 0, 2, and 4.
-/
DefinitionDoc Finset.image as "Finset.image"

/-- `Finset.mem_image` states that `y âˆˆ s.image f â†” âˆƒ x âˆˆ s, f x = y`.

## Syntax
```
rw [Finset.mem_image]       -- in the goal
rw [Finset.mem_image] at h  -- in a hypothesis
```

## When to use it
When the goal or a hypothesis involves `y âˆˆ s.image f`.
After rewriting, the goal becomes an existential that you solve
with `use`.

## Example
```
-- Goal: 6 âˆˆ (Finset.range 5).image (fun n => n * 2)
rw [Finset.mem_image]
-- Goal: âˆƒ x âˆˆ Finset.range 5, x * 2 = 6
use 3
```
-/
TheoremDoc Finset.mem_image as "Finset.mem_image" in "Finset"

TheoremTab "Finset"
NewDefinition Finset.image
NewTheorem Finset.mem_image

import Game.Metadata

World "PsetFin"
Level 1

Title "Two Distinct Witnesses"

Introduction "
# Problem Set: Fin Types

Welcome to your first problem set. You'll apply skills from
MeetFin, FinNavigation, and FinTuples to **fresh problems**
with sparser hints and no new definitions.

If you get stuck, ask yourself:
- Is the goal about **Fin elements being equal**? Try `ext`.
- Is the goal about **functions being equal**? Try `ext`.
- Is the goal about **values**? Try val lemmas + `omega`.
- Do you need to **enumerate cases**? Destructure + case split.
- Can you **reconstruct** a tuple? `cons_self_tail` or `vecSnoc_self_init`.

**Your task**: Find two distinct elements of `Fin 4` whose
values sum to 5.
"

/-- Two distinct Fin 4 elements whose values sum to 5. -/
Statement : âˆƒ i j : Fin 4, i â‰  j âˆ§ i.val + j.val = 5 := by
  Hint "Provide both witnesses at once with `use`.
  Which two numbers less than 4 sum to 5?"
  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `use âں¨2, by omegaâں©, âں¨3, by omegaâں©`."
  use âں¨2, by omegaâں©, âں¨3, by omegaâں©
  Hint "Split the conjunction with `constructor`."
  constructor
  آ· Hint "Prove the two elements are unequal.
    Recall the pattern from MeetFin: `intro h; cases h`."
    Hint (hidden := true) "`intro h` assumes they're equal,
    then `cases h` sees the values differ and closes the goal."
    intro h; cases h
  آ· Hint (hidden := true) "Both sides compute to 5. Use `rfl`."
    rfl

Conclusion "
Two skills combined on a fresh problem:
- `use âں¨k, by omegaâں©` for existential witnesses
- `intro h; cases h` for Fin inequality between concrete literals

The double `use` syntax â€” `use expr1, expr2` â€” fills
multiple existentials in one step.
"

     6â†’Title "Compound Membership"
     7â†’
     8â†’Introduction "
     9â†’# Problem Set: Finsets
    10â†’
    11â†’Welcome to the finset problem set.
    12â†’
    13â†’**Your task**: Prove that $5$ belongs to the intersection of two sets:
    14â†’the odd numbers in `range 8`, and the literal set $\\{3, 5, 7\\}$.
    15â†’
    16â†’Think about which `mem_*` lemmas unwrap each layer of the goal.
    17â†’"
    18â†’
    19â†’/-- 5 is in the odd numbers of range 8 intersected with {3, 5, 7}. -/
    20â†’Statement : (5 : â„•) âˆˆ (Finset.range 8).filter (fun n => n % 2 = 1) âˆ© ({3, 5, 7} : Finset â„•) := by
    21â†’  Hint (hidden := true) "Use `rw [Finset.mem_inter]` to split the intersection
    22â†’  membership into two parts, then `constructor`."
    23â†’  rw [Finset.mem_inter]
    24â†’  constructor
    25â†’  -- 5 âˆˆ (range 8).filter (odd)
    26â†’  آ· Hint (hidden := true) "Use `rw [Finset.mem_filter]` then `constructor`.
    27â†’    First part: `rw [Finset.mem_range]; omega`.
    28â†’    Second part: `rfl`."
    29â†’    rw [Finset.mem_filter]
    30â†’    constructor
    31â†’    آ· rw [Finset.mem_range]
    32â†’      omega
    33â†’    آ· rfl
    34â†’  -- 5 âˆˆ {3, 5, 7}
    35â†’  آ· Hint (hidden := true) "Peel inserts with `rw [Finset.mem_insert]` and
    36â†’    `left`/`right` until you reach `5 = 5`, then `rfl`."
    37â†’    rw [Finset.mem_insert]
    38â†’    right
    39â†’    rw [Finset.mem_insert]
    40â†’    left
    41â†’    rfl
    42â†’
    43â†’Conclusion "
    44â†’Two skills combined on a fresh surface:
    45â†’- `mem_inter` splits intersection membership into a conjunction
    46â†’- `mem_filter` splits filter membership into container + predicate
    47â†’- `mem_insert` peels literal finsets one element at a time
    48â†’"

import Game.Metadata

World "PsetFinset"
Level 1

Title "Compound Membership"

Introduction "
# Problem Set: Finsets

Welcome to the finset problem set.

**Your task**: Prove that $5$ belongs to the intersection of two sets:
the odd numbers in `range 8`, and the literal set $\\{3, 5, 7\\}$.

Think about which `mem_*` lemmas unwrap each layer of the goal.
"

/-- 5 is in the odd numbers of range 8 intersected with {3, 5, 7}. -/
Statement : (5 : â„•) âˆˆ (Finset.range 8).filter (fun n => n % 2 = 1) âˆ© ({3, 5, 7} : Finset â„•) := by
  Hint (hidden := true) "Use `rw [Finset.mem_inter]` to split the intersection
  membership into two parts, then `constructor`."
  rw [Finset.mem_inter]
  constructor
  -- 5 âˆˆ (range 8).filter (odd)
  آ· Hint (hidden := true) "Use `rw [Finset.mem_filter]` then `constructor`.
    First part: `rw [Finset.mem_range]; omega`.
    Second part: `rfl`."
    rw [Finset.mem_filter]
    constructor
    آ· rw [Finset.mem_range]
      omega
    آ· rfl
  -- 5 âˆˆ {3, 5, 7}
  آ· Hint (hidden := true) "Peel inserts with `rw [Finset.mem_insert]` and
    `left`/`right` until you reach `5 = 5`, then `rfl`."
    rw [Finset.mem_insert]
    right
    rw [Finset.mem_insert]
    left
    rfl

Conclusion "
Two skills combined on a fresh surface:
- `mem_inter` splits intersection membership into a conjunction
- `mem_filter` splits filter membership into container + predicate
- `mem_insert` peels literal finsets one element at a time
"

import Game.Metadata

World "Cardinality"
Level 1

Title "What Is Cardinality?"

Introduction "
# Counting Elements: Finset.card

You've been working with finsets â€” building them, testing membership,
taking unions and intersections, computing images. But you haven't yet
asked the most basic counting question: **how many elements does a
finset contain?**

The answer is `Finset.card` (or `.card` in dot notation). For any
finset `s`, the expression `s.card` is a natural number giving the
number of elements in `s`.

The simplest fact: the empty finset has zero elements.

$$|\\emptyset| = 0$$

In Lean: `Finset.card_empty : (âˆ… : Finset خ±).card = 0`

**Your task**: Prove that the empty finset of natural numbers has
cardinality zero.
"

/-- The empty finset has zero elements. -/
Statement : (âˆ… : Finset â„•).card = 0 := by
  Hint "The goal is `(âˆ… : Finset â„•).card = 0`. This is exactly
  what `Finset.card_empty` says."
  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `exact Finset.card_empty`."
  exact Finset.card_empty

Conclusion "
`Finset.card_empty` is the base case for cardinality: the empty set
has zero elements. This is the starting point for building up
cardinality of larger sets â€” just as `âˆ…` was the starting point for
building finsets by `insert`.

In plain math: $|\\emptyset| = 0$. This is the definition that makes
inductive cardinality arguments work.
"

/-- `Finset.card_empty` states that `(âˆ… : Finset خ±).card = 0`.

## Syntax
```
exact Finset.card_empty
rw [Finset.card_empty]
```

## When to use it
When the goal or a hypothesis involves the cardinality of the empty
finset.
-/
TheoremDoc Finset.card_empty as "Finset.card_empty" in "Card"

TheoremTab "Card"
NewTheorem Finset.card_empty

1â†’import Game.Metadata
     2â†’
     3â†’World "AbstractionLadder"
     4â†’Level 1
     5â†’
     6â†’Title "Meet the List"
     7â†’
     8â†’Introduction "
     9â†’# The Abstraction Ladder: List â†’ Multiset â†’ Finset
    10â†’
    11â†’You've been working with `Finset` â€” finite sets with no duplicates and no
    12â†’ordering. But where do finsets come from? What's underneath?
    13â†’
    14â†’The answer is a **three-layer abstraction**:
    15â†’
    16â†’| Layer | Type | Ordered? | Duplicates? |
    17â†’|---|---|---|---|
    18â†’| Bottom | `List خ±` | yes | yes |
    19â†’| Middle | `Multiset خ±` | no | yes |
    20â†’| Top | `Finset خ±` | no | no |
    21â†’
    22â†’Each layer *forgets* something:
    23â†’- `List â†’ Multiset` forgets **order** (by quotienting by permutations)
    24â†’- `Multiset â†’ Finset` forgets **duplicates** (by deduplication)
    25â†’
    26â†’**Why does Mathlib need all three layers?** Consider two scenarios:
    27â†’- **Shopping cart**: You buy 2 apples and 1 banana. The *order* you
    28â†’  added items doesn't matter, but the *quantities* do. You need a
    29â†’  **multiset**: `{apple, apple, banana}`.
    30â†’- **Ingredient list**: A recipe calls for apples and bananas. You
    31â†’  don't care *how many* of each â€” just *which* ingredients appear.
    32â†’  You need a **finset**: `{apple, banana}`.
    33â†’
    34â†’Lists alone can't express 'order doesn't matter.' Finsets alone
    35â†’can't track quantities. Each layer solves a different problem, and
    36â†’the conversions between them let you move to whichever level has
    37â†’the right tools for your current proof.
    38â†’
    39â†’This world climbs the ladder from the bottom. We start with lists.
    40â†’
    41â†’A `List خ±` is exactly what you'd expect: an ordered sequence of elements.
    42â†’You write them with square brackets: `[1, 2, 3]`.
    43â†’
    44â†’Under the hood, a list is built from two constructors:
    45â†’- `[]` â€” the empty list
    46â†’- `a :: l` â€” `a` prepended to list `l` ('cons')
    47â†’
    48â†’So `[1, 2, 3]` is sugar for `1 :: 2 :: 3 :: []`.
    49â†’
    50â†’The length of a list counts its elements:
    51â†’- `List.length_nil : [].length = 0`
    52â†’- `List.length_cons : (a :: l).length = l.length + 1`
    53â†’
    54â†’**Your task**: Given a list `l` of known length, determine the length
    55â†’of `a :: l`.
    56â†’"
    57â†’
    58â†’/-- Prepending an element to a list increases its length by 1. -/
    59â†’Statement (a : â„•) (l : List â„•) (h : l.length = 3) : (a :: l).length = 4 := by
    60â†’  Hint "The goal involves `(a :: l).length`. Use `List.length_cons` to
    61â†’  rewrite this as `l.length + 1`."
    62â†’  rw [List.length_cons]
    63â†’  Hint "The goal is now `l.length + 1 = 4`. Use the hypothesis `h` to
    64â†’  substitute `l.length` with `3`."
    65â†’  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [h]`."
    66â†’  rw [h]
    67â†’
    68â†’Conclusion "
    69â†’`List.length_cons` is one of the two base facts about list length (the
    70â†’other being `List.length_nil : [].length = 0`). Together they define
    71â†’length by recursion on the list structure.
    72â†’
    73â†’In plain math: if a sequence has $n$ elements, prepending one gives
    74â†’$n + 1$ elements.
    75â†’
    76â†’**Reusable recipe**: When you see a goal about `(a :: l).length`,
    77â†’reach for `List.length_cons`.
    78â†’"
    79â†’
    80â†’/-- `List.length_cons` states that `(a :: l).length = l.length + 1`.
    81â†’
    82â†’## Syntax
    83â†’```
    84â†’exact List.length_cons
    85â†’rw [List.length_cons]
    86â†’```
    87â†’
    88â†’## When to use it
    89â†’When the goal involves the length of a cons'd list `(a :: l).length`.
    90â†’-/
    91â†’TheoremDoc List.length_cons as "List.length_cons" in "List"
    92â†’
    93â†’/-- `List.length_nil` states that `[].length = 0`.
    94â†’
    95â†’## Syntax
    96â†’```
    97â†’exact List.length_nil
    98â†’rw [List.length_nil]
    99â†’```
   100â†’
   101â†’## When to use it
   102â†’When the goal involves the length of the empty list.
   103â†’-/
   104â†’TheoremDoc List.length_nil as "List.length_nil" in "List"
   105â†’
   106â†’/-- `List` is an ordered sequence of elements built from `[]` (empty)
   107â†’and `a :: l` (cons). Lists can contain duplicates and order matters.
   108â†’
   109â†’## Notation
   110â†’```
   111â†’[1, 2, 3]    -- sugar for 1 :: 2 :: 3 :: []
   112â†’a :: l       -- prepend a to list l
   113â†’```
   114â†’-/
   115â†’DefinitionDoc List as "List"
   116â†’
   117â†’/-- `List.length` returns the number of elements in a list.
   118â†’
   119â†’## Key lemmas
   120â†’- `List.length_nil : [].length = 0`
   121â†’- `List.length_cons : (a :: l).length = l.length + 1`
   122â†’- `List.length_append : (lâ‚پ ++ lâ‚‚).length = lâ‚پ.length + lâ‚‚.length`
   123â†’-/
   124â†’DefinitionDoc List.length as "List.length"
   125â†’
   126â†’TheoremTab "List"
   127â†’NewDefinition List List.length
   128â†’NewTheorem List.length_cons List.length_nil
   129â†’
   130â†’DisabledTactic trivial آ«decideآ» native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto linarith
   131â†’

import Game.Metadata

World "AbstractionLadder"
Level 1

Title "Meet the List"

Introduction "
# The Abstraction Ladder: List â†’ Multiset â†’ Finset

You've been working with `Finset` â€” finite sets with no duplicates and no
ordering. But where do finsets come from? What's underneath?

The answer is a **three-layer abstraction**:

| Layer | Type | Ordered? | Duplicates? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bottom | `List خ±` | yes | yes |
| Middle | `Multiset خ±` | no | yes |
| Top | `Finset خ±` | no | no |

Each layer *forgets* something:
- `List â†’ Multiset` forgets **order** (by quotienting by permutations)
- `Multiset â†’ Finset` forgets **duplicates** (by deduplication)

**Why does Mathlib need all three layers?** Consider two scenarios:
- **Shopping cart**: You buy 2 apples and 1 banana. The *order* you
  added items doesn't matter, but the *quantities* do. You need a
  **multiset**: `{apple, apple, banana}`.
- **Ingredient list**: A recipe calls for apples and bananas. You
  don't care *how many* of each â€” just *which* ingredients appear.
  You need a **finset**: `{apple, banana}`.

Lists alone can't express 'order doesn't matter.' Finsets alone
can't track quantities. Each layer solves a different problem, and
the conversions between them let you move to whichever level has
the right tools for your current proof.

This world climbs the ladder from the bottom. We start with lists.

A `List خ±` is exactly what you'd expect: an ordered sequence of elements.
You write them with square brackets: `[1, 2, 3]`.

Under the hood, a list is built from two constructors:
- `[]` â€” the empty list
- `a :: l` â€” `a` prepended to list `l` ('cons')

So `[1, 2, 3]` is sugar for `1 :: 2 :: 3 :: []`.

The length of a list counts its elements:
- `List.length_nil : [].length = 0`
- `List.length_cons : (a :: l).length = l.length + 1`

**Your task**: Given a list `l` of known length, determine the length
of `a :: l`.
"

/-- Prepending an element to a list increases its length by 1. -/
Statement (a : â„•) (l : List â„•) (h : l.length = 3) : (a :: l).length = 4 := by
  Hint "The goal involves `(a :: l).length`. Use `List.length_cons` to
  rewrite this as `l.length + 1`."
  rw [List.length_cons]
  Hint "The goal is now `l.length + 1 = 4`. Use the hypothesis `h` to
  substitute `l.length` with `3`."
  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `rw [h]`."
  rw [h]

Conclusion "
`List.length_cons` is one of the two base facts about list length (the
other being `List.length_nil : [].length = 0`). Together they define
length by recursion on the list structure.

In plain math: if a sequence has $n$ elements, prepending one gives
$n + 1$ elements.

**Reusable recipe**: When you see a goal about `(a :: l).length`,
reach for `List.length_cons`.
"

/-- `List.length_cons` states that `(a :: l).length = l.length + 1`.

## Syntax
```
exact List.length_cons
rw [List.length_cons]
```

## When to use it
When the goal involves the length of a cons'd list `(a :: l).length`.
-/
TheoremDoc List.length_cons as "List.length_cons" in "List"

/-- `List.length_nil` states that `[].length = 0`.

## Syntax
```
exact List.length_nil
rw [List.length_nil]
```

## When to use it
When the goal involves the length of the empty list.
-/
TheoremDoc List.length_nil as "List.length_nil" in "List"

/-- `List` is an ordered sequence of elements built from `[]` (empty)
and `a :: l` (cons). Lists can contain duplicates and order matters.

## Notation
```
[1, 2, 3]    -- sugar for 1 :: 2 :: 3 :: []
a :: l       -- prepend a to list l
```
-/
DefinitionDoc List as "List"

/-- `List.length` returns the number of elements in a list.

## Key lemmas
- `List.length_nil : [].length = 0`
- `List.length_cons : (a :: l).length = l.length + 1`
- `List.length_append : (lâ‚پ ++ lâ‚‚).length = lâ‚پ.length + lâ‚‚.length`
-/
DefinitionDoc List.length as "List.length"

TheoremTab "List"
NewDefinition List List.length
NewTheorem List.length_cons List.length_nil

DisabledTactic trivial آ«decideآ» native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto linarith

1â†’import Game.Metadata
     2â†’
     3â†’World "Fintype"
     4â†’Level 1
     5â†’
     6â†’Title "What is Fintype?"
     7â†’
     8â†’Introduction "
     9â†’# Fintype: The Typeclass of Finite Types
    10â†’
    11â†’In the Cardinality world you used `Finset.card_univ` and `Fintype.card_fin`
    12â†’to count elements. But what made those tools work? The answer is a
    13â†’**typeclass** called `Fintype`.
    14â†’
    15â†’`Fintype خ±` is Lean's way of saying: *the type `خ±` has finitely many
    16â†’elements, and I know exactly what they all are.*
    17â†’
    18â†’When `خ±` has a `Fintype` instance:
    19â†’- `Finset.univ : Finset خ±` exists (the finset of ALL elements)
    20â†’- `Fintype.card خ± : â„•` is defined (the total count)
    21â†’
    22â†’The simplest example: `Fin n` has a `Fintype` instance for every `n`.
    23â†’The theorem `Fintype.card_fin n` says `Fintype.card (Fin n) = n`.
    24â†’
    25â†’**Important warning**: Not every type is finite! The natural numbers `â„•`
    26â†’have **no** `Fintype` instance. Writing `Fintype.card â„•` is a type error â€”
    27â†’Lean will say it cannot find a `Fintype â„•` instance. If you've only worked
    28â†’with `Fin n`, this may be surprising: finiteness is a *property* that some
    29â†’types have, not a universal given.
    30â†’
    31â†’**Your task**: Use `Fintype.card_fin` to compute the cardinality of `Fin 7`.
    32â†’"
    33â†’
    34â†’/-- `Fin 7` has exactly 7 elements. -/
    35â†’Statement : Fintype.card (Fin 7) = 7 := by
    36â†’  Branch
    37â†’    rfl
    38â†’  Hint "Use `rw [Fintype.card_fin]` to rewrite the cardinality of
    39â†’  `Fin 7` to `7`. (Note: `rfl` also works here because the cardinality
    40â†’  is definitionally equal to 7. But practice using `card_fin` â€” you'll
    41â†’  need the `rw` technique when the types become parametric.)"
    42â†’  rw [Fintype.card_fin]
    43â†’
    44â†’Conclusion "
    45â†’`Fintype.card_fin` is the bridge between the type `Fin n` and the
    46â†’number `n`. You used this in the Cardinality world (inside `card_univ`),
    47â†’but now you're seeing it as part of a larger system: the `Fintype` typeclass.
    48â†’
    49â†’| Tool | Type | What it says |
    50â†’|---|---|---|
    51â†’| `Fintype خ±` | typeclass | `خ±` has finitely many elements |
    52â†’| `Fintype.card خ±` | `â„•` | the number of elements of `خ±` |
    53â†’| `Fintype.card_fin n` | equation | `Fintype.card (Fin n) = n` |
    54â†’
    55â†’Next: `Fin n` isn't the only finite type. `Bool` is finite too.
    56â†’"
    57â†’
    58â†’/-- `Fintype خ±` is a typeclass that asserts the type `خ±` has finitely
    59â†’many elements. When `خ±` has a `Fintype` instance, `Finset.univ : Finset خ±`
    60â†’and `Fintype.card خ± : â„•` are available.
    61â†’
    62â†’## Key facts
    63â†’- `Fintype.card خ±` counts the elements of `خ±`
    64â†’- `Finset.univ` is the finset of all elements
    65â†’- Not every type is finite â€” `â„•` has no `Fintype` instance
    66â†’
    67â†’## Example
    68â†’```
    69â†’Fintype.card (Fin 5) = 5    -- via Fintype.card_fin
    70â†’Fintype.card Bool = 2        -- Bool has two elements
    71â†’```
    72â†’-/
    73â†’DefinitionDoc Fintype as "Fintype"
    74â†’
    75â†’/-- `Fintype.card خ±` returns the number of elements of a finite type `خ±`.
    76â†’
    77â†’## Syntax
    78â†’```
    79â†’Fintype.card خ±    -- where خ± has a Fintype instance
    80â†’```
    81â†’
    82â†’## When to use it
    83â†’When you need to talk about how many elements a type has, rather than
    84â†’how many elements a particular finset contains.
    85â†’
    86â†’## Connection to Finset.card

import Game.Metadata

World "Fintype"
Level 1

Title "What is Fintype?"

Introduction "
# Fintype: The Typeclass of Finite Types

In the Cardinality world you used `Finset.card_univ` and `Fintype.card_fin`
to count elements. But what made those tools work? The answer is a
**typeclass** called `Fintype`.

`Fintype خ±` is Lean's way of saying: *the type `خ±` has finitely many
elements, and I know exactly what they all are.*

When `خ±` has a `Fintype` instance:
- `Finset.univ : Finset خ±` exists (the finset of ALL elements)
- `Fintype.card خ± : â„•` is defined (the total count)

The simplest example: `Fin n` has a `Fintype` instance for every `n`.
The theorem `Fintype.card_fin n` says `Fintype.card (Fin n) = n`.

**Important warning**: Not every type is finite! The natural numbers `â„•`
have **no** `Fintype` instance. Writing `Fintype.card â„•` is a type error â€”
Lean will say it cannot find a `Fintype â„•` instance. If you've only worked
with `Fin n`, this may be surprising: finiteness is a *property* that some
types have, not a universal given.

**Your task**: Use `Fintype.card_fin` to compute the cardinality of `Fin 7`.
"

/-- `Fin 7` has exactly 7 elements. -/
Statement : Fintype.card (Fin 7) = 7 := by
  Branch
    rfl
  Hint "Use `rw [Fintype.card_fin]` to rewrite the cardinality of
  `Fin 7` to `7`. (Note: `rfl` also works here because the cardinality
  is definitionally equal to 7. But practice using `card_fin` â€” you'll
  need the `rw` technique when the types become parametric.)"
  rw [Fintype.card_fin]

Conclusion "
`Fintype.card_fin` is the bridge between the type `Fin n` and the
number `n`. You used this in the Cardinality world (inside `card_univ`),
but now you're seeing it as part of a larger system: the `Fintype` typeclass.

| Tool | Type | What it says |
|---|---|---|
| `Fintype خ±` | typeclass | `خ±` has finitely many elements |
| `Fintype.card خ±` | `â„•` | the number of elements of `خ±` |
| `Fintype.card_fin n` | equation | `Fintype.card (Fin n) = n` |

Next: `Fin n` isn't the only finite type. `Bool` is finite too.
"

/-- `Fintype خ±` is a typeclass that asserts the type `خ±` has finitely
many elements. When `خ±` has a `Fintype` instance, `Finset.univ : Finset خ±`
and `Fintype.card خ± : â„•` are available.

## Key facts
- `Fintype.card خ±` counts the elements of `خ±`
- `Finset.univ` is the finset of all elements
- Not every type is finite â€” `â„•` has no `Fintype` instance

## Example
```
Fintype.card (Fin 5) = 5    -- via Fintype.card_fin
Fintype.card Bool = 2        -- Bool has two elements
```
-/
DefinitionDoc Fintype as "Fintype"

/-- `Fintype.card خ±` returns the number of elements of a finite type `خ±`.

## Syntax
```
Fintype.card خ±    -- where خ± has a Fintype instance
```

## When to use it
When you need to talk about how many elements a type has, rather than
how many elements a particular finset contains.

## Connection to Finset.card
- `Finset.card s` counts elements of a specific finset `s`
- `Fintype.card خ±` counts ALL elements of the type `خ±`
- They're connected: `Finset.card_univ : (Finset.univ).card = Fintype.card خ±`
-/
DefinitionDoc Fintype.card as "Fintype.card"

NewDefinition Fintype Fintype.card

TheoremTab "Fintype"

DisabledTactic trivial آ«decideآ» native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto linarith

     7â†’
     8â†’Introduction "
     9â†’# Lists Are Ordered â€” Permutations Ignore Order
    10â†’
    11â†’Two lists with the same elements in different orders are **not** equal:
    12â†’`[1, 2, 3] â‰  [2, 1, 3]`. Order is part of a list's identity.
    13â†’
    14â†’But sometimes we want to say 'these lists have the same elements,
    15â†’just rearranged.' That's `List.Perm` â€” the permutation relation.
    16â†’
    17â†’We write `lâ‚پ ~ lâ‚‚` (or `lâ‚پ.Perm lâ‚‚`) to mean `lâ‚پ` is a permutation
    18â†’of `lâ‚‚`.
    19â†’
    20â†’The key building blocks:
    21â†’
    22â†’- **`List.Perm.swap x y l`** â€” swapping two adjacent elements gives
    23â†’  a permutation: `[y, x] ++ l ~ [x, y] ++ l`
    24â†’- **`List.Perm.cons a h`** â€” if `lâ‚پ ~ lâ‚‚`, then `a :: lâ‚پ ~ a :: lâ‚‚`
    25â†’  (prepending the same element preserves the permutation)
    26â†’- **`List.Perm.trans hâ‚پ hâ‚‚`** â€” if `lâ‚پ ~ lâ‚‚` and `lâ‚‚ ~ lâ‚ƒ`,
    27â†’  then `lâ‚پ ~ lâ‚ƒ` (chain permutations)
    28â†’
    29â†’**Your task**: Prove that `[2, 1, 3]` is a permutation of `[1, 2, 3]`.
    30â†’This is a single swap of the first two elements.
    31â†’"
    32â†’
    33â†’/-- Swapping the first two elements gives a permutation. -/
    34â†’Statement : List.Perm [2, 1, 3] [1, 2, 3] := by
    35â†’  Hint "The lists `[2, 1, 3]` and `[1, 2, 3]` differ only in the order
    36â†’  of the first two elements. This is exactly `List.Perm.swap 1 2 [3]`."
    37â†’  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `exact List.Perm.swap 1 2 [3]`.
    38â†’  This says: swapping `1` and `2` in front of `[3]` gives
    39â†’  `[2, 1, 3] ~ [1, 2, 3]`."
    40â†’  exact List.Perm.swap 1 2 [3]
    41â†’
    42â†’Conclusion "
    43â†’`List.Perm.swap` is the atomic permutation move: swap two adjacent
    44â†’elements. Every permutation can be built from a sequence of swaps.
    45â†’
    46â†’The type signature: `List.Perm.swap x y l : (y :: x :: l).Perm (x :: y :: l)`.
    47â†’
    48â†’Note the argument order: `swap x y l` produces `[y, x, ...] ~ [x, y, ...]`.
    49â†’The elements appear **reversed** on the left side.
    50â†’
    51â†’**Why permutations matter for the ladder**: The `Multiset` type is
    52â†’defined as the quotient `List خ± / Perm`. Two lists that are
    53â†’permutations of each other become the **same** multiset. This is
    54â†’how order gets forgotten.
    55â†’"
    56â†’
    57â†’/-- `List.Perm` is the permutation relation on lists. `lâ‚پ.Perm lâ‚‚`
    58â†’(notation: `lâ‚پ ~ lâ‚‚`) means `lâ‚پ` is a rearrangement of `lâ‚‚`.
    59â†’
    60â†’## Building permutations
    61â†’- `List.Perm.swap x y l : (y :: x :: l).Perm (x :: y :: l)` â€” swap adjacent
    62â†’- `List.Perm.cons a h : (a :: lâ‚پ).Perm (a :: lâ‚‚)` from `h : lâ‚پ.Perm lâ‚‚`
    63â†’- `List.Perm.trans hâ‚پ hâ‚‚` â€” chain two permutations
    64â†’
    65â†’## Key facts
    66â†’- `List.Perm.length_eq : lâ‚پ.Perm lâ‚‚ â†’ lâ‚پ.length = lâ‚‚.length`
    67â†’- `Multiset.coe_eq_coe : â†‘lâ‚پ = â†‘lâ‚‚ â†” lâ‚پ.Perm lâ‚‚`
    68â†’-/

    72â†’

    75â†’exact List.Perm.swap x y l
    76â†’```
    77â†’

    79â†’When two lists differ only by a swap of two adjacent elements.
    80â†’
    81â†’## Warning
    82â†’The arguments `x y` appear in the *reverse* order on the left:
    83â†’`swap x y l` gives `[y, x, ...] ~ [x, y, ...]`.
    84â†’-/
